entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Regulatory Outlook 2026 and Onwards
entry.content:
A few weeks ago, the OECD released its Regulatory compliance costs and productivity: new task-based evidence and its findings are unequivocal: the size and complexity of overall regulation have increased in OECD countries.

In addition to circumstantial evidence, the study also based its conclusions based on an estimated share of labour resources devoted to regulation-related tasks. As per the report, in the USA “the wage share devoted to compliance tasks increases from 4.0% in 2012 to 4.2% in 2024, while remaining stable at about 4.5% in Australia. In Europe, an employment-based indicator rises from 3.7% in 2011 to 3.9% in 2023”.

In summary, this means that over 4% of aggregate wages are spent on regulatory compliance tasks in the US and Australia and nearly that figure in Europe.

Illustrating this inflationary trend, the authors cite a few examples such as:  

  • The total number of restrictions in the US Code of Federal Regulations went from 400 thousand restrictive words in 1970 to more than 1 million today
  • In the EU, the number of Commission legislative proposals has increased from 374 during 1999- 2004 to 431 during 2019-2024 while the average length of each proposal has almost doubled and their language has also become increasingly complex

These trends show that there was a parallel evolution in those various geographies. At least until recently.

Indeed, whereas the previous decades had been marked with some level of compliance harmonization across the globe where internal control, codes of conduct or financial transparency requirements had been enforced in many countries with great similarities, it seems the world is at a fracture point with strong divergences between major economic blocks.

In a striking opposition, the European Union continues on its “Brussels effect” trajectory with the idea that regulations can better protect consumers and market competitiveness, whereas there is a perceived rollback of the US on compliance enforcements and even a move towards de-regulation with the idea that less business constraints is overall more beneficiary.

For organizations operating globally, this new reality will have to be considered for sure, but, if the US potentially slows down its regulatory inflation, it’s undeniable that the European Union will follow but other major countries such as China, Australia, Singapore and others will not. And there are various areas in which regulatory pressure will continue to increase.

In this blog, I have tried to summarize the findings of 30+ studies to identify the key compliance areas that will require attention from 2026 onwards due to new or more stringent mandates:

As of last year already, Cybersecurity is top of mind, but it is this year very much intertwined with a more all-encompassing digital resilience perspective.

Legislation on digital (or cyber) resilience set minimum requirements on how an organization must prepare for, withstand, and recover from disruptions that affect its digital systems, data and services, including cyberattacks, outages, and third‑party failures.

An example here would be EU’s Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA).

It is to be noted that a recent “subcategory” is being introduced here: Data Use and Access Acts that regulate the sharing of data generated by IoT devices and industrial machines - including non-personal data.

When it comes to AI governance, regulations are designed to establish rules for the ethical development, deployment, and oversight of AI systems to mitigate risks like bias, privacy violations, and lack of transparency. A key intent is to create an accountability for the AI models delivered – especially when used in customer context (i.e. either to deliver a service or make an informed decision on whether to grant a service to a customer) and to ensure human oversight in critical decisions.

The EU Artificial Intelligence Act is the world's first comprehensive AI law, but the United States has also decided for similar legislation – albeit decentralized – with the Colorado's AI Act or the California AI Transparency and Employment Laws for example.

Third-party and supply chain regulations mandate organizations to identify, assess, monitor, and mitigate operational risks from their vendors and suppliers, but also their extended fourth parties, to prevent disruptions, data breaches, or compliance failures.

Some of the regulations in this category can be cybersecurity focused – such as NIS2 Directive for instance, others will be very much in the “physical” world such as the Modern Slavery Act in the UK, the Duty of Vigilance Law in France or the Supply Chain Due Diligence Act in Germany.

For years, digital asset markets operated in a regulatory grey zone. Digital and payments regulations are meant to address this void and govern electronic transactions, data flows, and financial innovations such instant payments, open banking, and digital identities to ensure security, competition, and consumer protection.

Requirements related to Payment Services Directive 3 in Europe focused on fraud prevention, consumer rights and innovation is illustrative of this category focused on digital services.

Some frameworks have also been specifically focused on certain digital asset types such as the Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation regulating crypto-assets not covered by traditional financial laws.

Legislative requirements in this category are meant to ensure that companies operate responsibly and sustainably. As a result, these standards are designed to measure a company's impact on the environment (climate change, natural resource and waste management), its social responsibilities (diversity and inclusion, protection of human rights), and its governance practices (board diversity, compensation model).

The International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS), Sustainability Disclosure Standards, Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) standards or the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) are good illustrations of associated mandates.

For many institutions, ESG reporting remains isolated, disconnected from the systems that manage risk and compliance. With the mandates mentioned just above, regulators are closing that gap, expecting firms to show how their sustainability performance is linked to governance, strategy, and accountability.

If Digital Assets and Payments regulations already included a focus on fraud detection and deterrence, financial crime regulations are legal frameworks designed to prevent, detect, and report all illicit activities such as money laundering, terrorist financing, sanctions evasion, bribery, and proliferation financing.

Requirements in this category are multiform from due diligence mandate to transaction monitoring, suspicious activity reporting, risk assessments, and record-keeping as a basis for all.

In addition to Anti-Money Laundering laws such as the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act in Australia or the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act in Malaysia, there are of course also the numerous sanctions lists: Denied Persons List, Non-proliferation Sanctions, Consolidated List, List of Statutorily Debarred Parties, Military End User List, OFAC Non-SDN Sanctions, Specially Designated Nationals List, Unverified List, Consolidated United Nations Security Council Sanctions List of Individuals and Entities… to name but a few!

Regulations in this last category set minimum behavioral standards by promoting personal responsibility, clear governance, and proper conduct standards to act with due integrity, reduce consumer harm, and enhance market integrity. The main requirements relate to taking reasonable steps to identify and manage conflicts of interest – especially for employees looking after customer interests or executives with roles of authority (Senior Managers). As its name suggests, the Senior Manager Regime in the UK is a representative of this matter.

What about you, what are the main regulatory requirements on your radar for the year(s) to come? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP GRC for SAP HANA
entry.content:
It’s here, yes, it’s really here! No, not the new iPhone but much better than that. The new SAP GRC for SAP HANA release!

Since March 6th, it is available in restricted shipment – meaning for customers in Early Adoption Care program but, this summer, in Q3, it will be generally available to all customers.

If you don’t believe me, then here is the official SAP Note 3709892 - Release Information Note for SAP GRC For SAP HANA

 

What is SAP GRC for SAP HANA?

 

SAP GRC for SAP HANA is the next evolution of SAP GRC, unifying SAP GRC 12.0 and SAP GRC 12.0 for S/4HANA into a single HANA-based platform for future innovations, and it will continue to be available both OnPremise and in the cloud.  

Built on 20 years of in market expertise, it replaces SAP GRC 12.0 “classic” versions (SAP Access Control, Process Control and Risk Management, UI masking and UI logging) and is the new version for SAP GRC for S/4HANA 12.0 products (including SAP Access Control for S/4HANA, SAP Process Control for S/4HANA and SAP Risk Management for S/4HANA), SAP Assurance and Compliance Software (SAP Business Integrity Screening and SAP Audit Management) and UI masking and UI logging for S/4HANA. 

This evolution delivers a unified platform to manage Governance, Risk, and Compliance requirements across business processes:

Fully cohesive with the SAP Business Suite with its native integration into SAP S/4HANA – the AI ERP, SAP Signavio – the single source of truth for business processes, Regulatory Insights – for regulatory surveillance, and SAP Analytics Cloud – for best-in-class reporting and visualization, data flows from one module to another seamlessly and establishes a glass pane where all risks are surfaced and tracked in a single unified yet modular platform for governance, control, risk management and data protection:

 

What’s new compared to previous versions?

 

SAP GRC for SAP HANA elevates the user experience by shifting from complex, context-heavy interfaces to modern, intuitive designs that prioritize simplicity and efficiency.

New dedicated SAP Fiori UIs, provide a consistent, user-friendly design across different devices and platforms. Boosting adoption and productivity.

Context and role-based, these apps deliver personalized interactions with relevant tiles adapted to user’s profile and needs.

Designed for organizations in all geographies, this new version is truly multilingual with 28 languages supported across all modules.

For Governance aspects, SAP GRC for SAP HANA integrates with SAP Identity Services and Entra ID to establish a single source of truth for user identity, enhancing security, compliance, and operational efficiency.

For Control and Compliance, the platform integrates to the Regulatory Insights service to enable 2nd line users to effortlessly update the control framework based on the latest regulatory information and the new sub-scenario for SAP Integration Suite with pre-delivered integrations extends the connectivity of Continuous Control Monitoring to SAP and non-SAP sources for hybrid landscape.

To increase transparency, security, compliance, and decision-making capabilities, Analytics Dashboard enable business users to evaluate and navigate their specific risk and compliance tasks efficiently and gain additional contextual insights.

The native integration of SAP GRC for SAP HANA to the SAP Analytics Cloud reporting engine also enables best-in-class visualization and reporting. As a next step, SAP GRC for SAP HANA also intends to activate secure and standardised data assets for cloud-based analytics and insights on SAP Business Data Cloud.

UI Data Protection logging and masking modules are now natively embedded in SAP GRC for SAP HANA. Strengthening the protection of data with configurable masking and blocking safeguards sensitive field values across SAP interfaces and secures operations by ensuring only authorized users can view or reveal protected information when needed.

Together, logging and masking reduce data-abuse risks, help organizations meet legal retention and privacy requirements, and empower data protection teams with easy access to meaningful insights.

A study by McKinsey concluded that 88% of companies “report regular AI use in at least one business function” at the end of 2025.

With GRC being at the crossroad of core business processes, multiple AI use cases have been already added to SAP GRC for SAP HANA, and more are planned before the end of the year.

Select examples of what has been delivered but also plans in progress include:

AI in Governance

 

User Augmented Access Management by integrating SAP’s conversational AI agent Joule for user access request processes

 

Key benefits:

·       Faster, more accurate requests with less manual effort

·       Enhanced user experience via AI-driven interaction

·       Improved consistency and compliance with role models

 

 

AI-supported sensitive fields advisor for masking to receive recommendations on personal data fields available in end-user applications, enabling organizations to secure sensitive information across multiple applications without manually collecting field details

 

Key benefits:

·       Increase coverage of sensitive data protection

·       Reduce manual effort, implementation and operational effort

·       Improve solution implementation experience

 

 

AI in Risk Management

 

AI-powered end-user support for key risk indicators and data configuration with Joule.

 

Key benefits:

·       Support KRI implementation and KRI business rule creation using Joule, with AI-driven suggestions for existing CDS views, queries, OData services, and source tables for customer-specific CDS view development.

 

 

AI in Compliance and Assurance

 

Intelligent self-diagnostic cockpit by automatically analysing the errors in continuous control monitoring and proposing resolution capabilities

 

Key benefits:

·       Conduct automated control jobs for self-diagnosing errors and providing solutions

·       Enhance operational efficiency and resilience with issue detection and problem-solving guidance

 

 

AI-supported Audit Report Summary by leveraging prompts to customize the output of the audit report summary

 

Key benefits:

·       Automate the generation of the audit report and customize the outcome

·       Use existing work programs, working papers, and findings as inputs for the AI prompts

 

 

Key take aways

 

SAP GRC for SAP HANA is a new evolution of SAP GRC solutions. This first 2026 release is already filled with enhancements, and more are planned.

Fully integrated into the SAP Business Suite, SAP GRC for SAP HANA creates a single continuously updated platform, automatically monitoring your core processes, governing access and protecting sensitive data.

With SAP GRC for SAP HANA, you can expect to achieve:

 

Early detection of issues and timely closure

 

Improved assurance transparency and compliance process efficiency

 

Data quality checks for finance, compliance and risk management purposes

Oh, and one last thing: keep an eye out for our next blog – Why Move to SAP GRC for SAP HANA which will be released in this same GRC Tuesdays series on March 31st.

Since this blog can’t be exhaustive without being too long, we have taken the liberty of selecting the enhancements that we felt were the most relevant. But many more have been added to SAP GRC for SAP HANA and are detailed on their respective SAP Notes:

  • What’s New in SAP Access Control for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP Process Control for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP Risk Management for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP Audit Management for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP Business Integrity Screening for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP UI Data Protection Logging for SAP HANA
  • What’s New in SAP UI Data Protection Masking for SAP HANA

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Governing the Emerging Collective Intelligence
entry.content:
Artificial intelligence is often described as a technological breakthrough. But what if it is something more fundamental? Today’s AI systems are trained on vast amounts of human-generated data, documents, transactions, conversations, and decisions. In doing so, they do not simply process information. They reorganise fragments of our shared intellectual and cultural history.

When AI systems retrieve and interpret this data, they surface not only knowledge, but also ambiguity, bias, contradiction, and unresolved human tension. What emerges is not purely artificial intelligence-it is a reflection of humanity itself. This raises a profound question: Are we building systems that replicate human reasoning, or systems that externalise our collective mind?

From Collective Unconscious to Data Lakes

Carl Jung introduced the idea of the collective unconscious—a deeper layer of shared psychological structures that shape how humans perceive, think, and act. Modern enterprises are, in many ways, building a technological equivalent. Data lakes, enterprise platforms, and AI systems aggregate vast amounts of collective experience—sales interactions, customer behaviour, operational decisions—into shared repositories. From these, patterns emerge. Insights form. Decisions are guided.

But there is a critical difference. The collective unconscious was invisible. Enterprise intelligence systems are not. They are queryable, operational, and increasingly autonomous. This shift transforms something deeply human into something actionable—and scalable.

The Mirror and the Shadow

If AI systems reflect our collective knowledge, they also reflect our limitations. They can amplify:

  • Biases embedded in historical data
  • Misaligned incentives within organisations
  • Incomplete or conflicting interpretations of reality

In this sense, AI is not just a tool. It is a mirror—one that shows both clarity and distortion. Without proper governance, this mirror does not simply reflect reality. It can reshape it.

From Intelligence to Responsibility

This is where the conversation must evolve. AI governance is often framed as a technical or regulatory concern—focused on compliance, accuracy, and control. These are necessary, but not sufficient. If AI represents an externalised form of collective intelligence, then governance becomes something more: AI governance is the stewardship of how human knowledge, decisions, and meaning are captured, interpreted, and amplified by machines.

This introduces new responsibilities for organisations:

  • Not only to manage data, but to understand what it represents
  • Not only to deploy AI, but to question what it reinforces
  • Not only to optimise outcomes, but to ensure alignment with human intent

The Enterprise Imperative

For enterprises, this shift is already happening. AI copilots, assistants, and agents are becoming embedded in daily workflows—supporting sellers, guiding decisions, and interpreting complex systems like compensation, forecasting, and customer engagement. These systems do more than automate tasks. They shape how people understand reality within the organisation. And that makes governance not optional—but foundational.

From SaaS to Governing Intelligence: The Role of Joule

Enterprise software is undergoing a fundamental transformation. For decades, SaaS systems were designed to store, process, and expose data. Their primary role was to provide structure, consistency, and transactional reliability across business processes. With the rise of AI, this role is expanding. Systems are no longer just systems of record or systems of engagement. They are becoming systems of interpretation—capable of explaining outcomes, guiding decisions, and interacting with users in natural language. This is where SAP Joule represents a significant shift. Joule is not simply an AI assistant embedded into enterprise applications. It acts as an interface to enterprise intelligence, connecting data, processes, and business context into a unified layer of understanding. But more importantly, Joule introduces the foundation for something new: A governed layer of enterprise intelligence.

As AI agents begin to interpret sensitive domains such as compensation, performance, customer interactions, and financial outcomes, the need for trusted interpretation becomes critical. This moves SAP’s role beyond delivering SaaS applications. It positions SAP as a provider of:

  • Context-aware intelligence, grounded in enterprise data
  • Traceable reasoning, connecting outputs back to underlying transactions
  • Governed AI interactions, aligned with business rules, policies, and intent

In this model, Joule is not only assisting users—it is operating within a controlled and explainable framework of enterprise knowledge. This is the transition:

  • From delivering applications
  • To enabling decisions
  • To govern how intelligence itself is produced and consumed within the enterprise

Why This Matters-As organisations adopt AI at scale, the risk is not only incorrect answers, but ungoverned meaning.

Without a governing layer, AI may generate plausible but ungrounded interpretations. Decisions may drift from policy and intent. Trust in enterprise systems may erode. With Joule and SAP’s AI foundation, there is an opportunity to ensure that:

  • Intelligence is grounded in enterprise truth
  • Outputs are explainable and auditable
  • AI becomes a trusted extension of the organisation, not an uncontrolled external layer

A Strategic Shift-This marks a broader transformation: From Software as a Service → to Governed Intelligence as a Capability

In this future, the value of enterprise platforms will not be defined only by the data they store—but by how responsibly they interpret and operationalise that data through AI.

A New Discipline

We are entering a new phase of AI adoption—one where the question is no longer what AI can do, but what it should represent. This requires a new discipline that connects:

  • Technology and psychology
  • Data and meaning
  • Automation and accountability

Because in the end, the systems we build will not only reflect our intelligence—They will reflect our values.

As enterprises continue to build and deploy AI, one question should remain at the centre: If AI becomes the voice of our collective intelligence—who ensures it speaks with clarity, responsibility, and truth?

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Why Move to SAP GRC for SAP HANA
entry.content:
What is SAP GRC for HANA?

 

Put simply: SAP GRC for SAP HANA is a new evolution of SAP GRC solutions and your path to a future proof GRC platform.

Fully integrated into the SAP Business Suite, SAP GRC for SAP HANA creates a single continuously updated platform, automatically monitoring your core processes, governing access and protecting sensitive data:

 

What is new in SAP GRC edition for HANA?

 

From user experience enhancements with new dedicated Fiori apps, to new capabilities, integration extensions and AI native features, a lot of improvements have already been released in the SAP GRC for SAP HANA version and more are planned.

For details on what is already available, please see the dedicated blog post GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP GRC for SAP HANA, released on March 17th 2026.

 

What are the benefits of SAP GRC edition for GRC HANA?

 

In addition to the enhancements delivered, there are also many other reasons to move to this new version, including:

Technical gains

 

More flexibility

Added flexibility fuelled by additional configurability and extended integration capabilities to SAP and non-SAP systems.

 

Easier deployment and maintenance

A unified suite with one software solution and one UI stack provides easier deployment – OnPremise or in cloud – and reduced ongoing maintenance complexity.

 

Ongoing compatibility

This new version, but also all the future ones, keep pace with operating systems, browsers, hardware, and integrations so the product continues to work well in a changing ecosystem.

 

Functional benefits

 

Better user experience

Unified and configurable usability across the entire GRC platform and, more widely, across SAP solutions.

 

Truly multilingual

28 languages are supported across all modules including: Arabic, Chinese (simplified), Traditional Chinese, Croatian, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese (Brazil), Romanian, Russian, Serbian, Slovak, Slovenian, Spanish, Swedish, Turkish, Ukrainian

 

Real‑time analytics

 

Intuitive analytical pages for key insights and augmented decision making thanks to the HANA database capabilities.

 

New capabilities

SAP GRC for SAP HANA is not just a new “skin”, it comes with a flurry of enhancements available to this version only and some are detailed in the blog GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP GRC for SAP HANA

Just as an excerpt, new features include:

o   Streamlined access role management

o   Flexible workflow management for controls and risks

o   New risk analysis type

o   Shared recommendations for different findings

o   Centralized data protection configurations

o   Alerting notifications when specific data is accessed

o   … and much, much more!

 

Native data protection

 

Centralized attribute-based access control thanks to now embedded UI data protection logging and masking capabilities.

 

Embedded AI

AI functionalities across solutions across all components of SAP GRC for HANA enabling:

o   Easier audit report creation with Generative AI

o   Regulation analysis and summarization with Large Language Modelling

o   Intelligent continuous control monitoring error analysis and resolution

o   Automated sensitive data identification for masking with AI

o   ... and much more!

 

Operational advantages

 

Strategic alignment

The new suite is fully rebuilt on SAP S/4HANA Foundation and SAP HANA, aligning with SAP's long-term tech roadmap. Moving to SAP GRC for SAP HANA aligns organizations with SAP’s broader S/4HANA and HANA‑centric roadmap, making it the recommended path for customers planning long‑term on‑premise or hybrid GRC landscapes.

 

Defined upgrade and migration path

Predefined upgrade paths for SAP GRC "for S/4HANA" customers and seamless migration for SAP GRC "classic" customers.

 

Included in your maintenance

SAP GRC for SAP HANA is already included in the maintenance fee for customers licensed on SAP GRC for S/4HANA modules.

 

A roadmap for the future

 

Futureproof platform

This new version protects your existing investment with a standard mainstream maintenance until 2040 accompanied by a long-term roadmap vision with enhancements regularly delivered until then.

 

Is there a cost associated to this license?

 

Since SAP GRC for SAP HANA is the new version of SAP GRC for SAP S/4HANA solutions, it is included in the standard maintenance for these products and there is no new material number or new commercialization. Organizations licensed for SAP GRC for S/4HANA solutions (such as SAP Access Control for S/4HANA for instance) and up to date on their maintenance will therefore be able to upgrade to the SAP GRC for SAP HANA version with no additional license fee.

Customers licensed on “classic” versions of these products will need to convert their licenses to the “S/4HANA” versions first to be able to benefit from this new SAP GRC for SAP HANA version. To do so, SAP Account Teams will be able to support and guide through the process.

 

How can I migrate to SAP GRC edition for SAP HANA?

 

Upgrade guides are already available from the individual product pages – also accessible in the SAP Help Portal for SAP GRC for SAP HANA:

 

  • Upgrade Guide for SAP Access Control for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP Process Control for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP Risk Management for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP Audit Management for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP Business Integrity Screening for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP UI Data Protection Logging for SAP HANA
  • Upgrade Guide for SAP UI Data Protection Masking for SAP HANA

 

When will it be available?

 

The Release to Customer for SAP GRC for SAP HANA – the initial availability to early adopters, has already happened on March 6th and General Availability, which is when the product is available to all customers, is planned for Q3/2026.

 

What’s next for this new platform?

 

As its name suggests, SAP GRC for SAP HANA is only the “2026” release. With mainstream maintenance currently planned until 2040 and continuous improvement cycles until then, many more enhancements but also use cases will be added:

 

As a matter of fact, the roadmap for each product is regularly updated as new enhancements are planned and it is published on the Elevate your governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) strategy page of the SAP Road Map Explorer tool. Bookmark this page to stay updated.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Governing Possible Future
entry.content:
In my previous post on 'Governing the Emerging Collective Intelligence', I explored an idea that has stayed with me: That AI is not just processing data -it is reorganising and externalising fragments of our collective knowledge, decisions, and experience. But what happens when that intelligence becomes operational inside a system like Sales Performance Management? Why did SPM always feel like the ideal space for AI to me? I’ve been asking myself this question for some time. SPM somehow brings everything together in a way that feels… almost as if it were designed for AI. There is a constant need for optimisation. There is a need for guided navigation, not just reporting. There is demand for real-time visibility. And there is always the question of prediction -what comes next, what will I earn, what should I focus on? It is not a static system. It is alive.

But I think the real reason is something deeper. SPM is one of the rare places where data does not stay abstract. It becomes personal. Every transaction, every rule, every adjustment - created by different people across the organisation - ultimately shapes a single outcome: compensation.

And that immediately raises questions: Why is my payout like this? Is this correct? What does it mean for me? What should I do next?

These are not just data questions. They are questions of interpretation. And now, increasingly, they are answered by AI. That’s why SPM feels like such a natural fit. Not because it “can use AI” - but because it already behaves like a system that is waiting for a cognitive layer.

At the same time, this realisation changes something. If AI starts explaining outcomes, guiding decisions, and influencing behaviour… then we are no longer just implementing AI in SPM. We are shaping how people understand their value, their performance, and their future.

Which makes me wonder: Are we building intelligent systems…or are we beginning to govern a form of collective intelligence?

If we look at it structurally, the core loop of such a system could be described as:

DATA → INTERPRETATION → DECISION → MOTIVATION → BEHAVIOUR → DATA

Mapped to SPM:

DATA->Transactions, credits, quotas, hierarchies

INTERPRETATION (AI enters here)->“Why did I earn this?” ->Assistants/agents

DECISION->Where to focus, what deals to prioritise

MOTIVATION->Incentives, fairness perception, trust

BEHAVIOUR->Sales actions, performance changes

→ feeding back into DATA

 And yet, something is still missing. A governing layer.

GOVERNANCE OF INTELLIGENCE /Rules & compensation logic/Data quality -the “system of truth”/AI explanations and boundaries/Fairness and transparency/Ownership — who defines the logic?

SPM is not just a system of record. It is behaving as a governed loop of collective intelligence.

And there is one more layer that I keep coming back to. Once we introduce forecasting — and especially this idea of “model as you go” -something shifts again. SPM is no longer just explaining the past. It is no longer only guiding present decisions. It starts to simulate the future.

What will I earn if I close this deal?

How would my payout change under a different plan?

What happens if I shift focus this quarter?

 At that point, the system becomes something else. Not just a system of interpretation. Not just a system of guidance. But a space where possible outcomes can be explored. Almost like a sandbox. And that raises an even deeper question. If forecasts influence decisions, and decisions influence behaviour…then those modelled futures don’t just stay hypothetical. They start shaping reality.

So now we are not only governing data. We are not only governing intelligence. We are beginning to govern possible futures.

 If we extend the model:

 Inner circle → Reality (what is happening)

Outer circle → Simulation (what could happen)

 AI sits between them. Governance surrounds both.

entry.title: 2026 Key Technical Updates for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
The SAP Commerce Cloud 2211 continuous innovation release model offers substantial benefits by ensuring faster access to the latest features, enhancements, and security updates. This approach allows for seamless and frequent updates, minimizing disruptions while maintaining a robust and high-performing e-commerce platform. You can take advantage of new innovations quickly, stay ahead of market demands, and maintain competitive agility, all while reducing the risk associated with traditional major version cycles.
 
To successfully operate and plan with SAP Commerce Cloud, we strongly recommend focusing on the following key areas: 
  1. Stay current with SAP Commerce Cloud monthly and Framework Update Releases
  2. Stay current on Composable Storefront and compatible with its dependencies (Node.js, Angular)
  3. Review validity and compatibility of all features, services, and updates (e.g. Solr, Extensions)

 

1 – Stay Current with SAP Commerce Cloud Monthly and Framework Update Releases

 
Staying up to date with the latest SAP Commerce Cloud and Composable Storefront releases ensures access to the latest enhancements, bug fixes, performance improvements, and security updates. 
 
To assist you in preparing for upcoming changes and implementations, SAP publishes the Notification of Key Technical Updates on the SAP Help Portal. This continuously updated documentation highlights when changes are available, including features and services that have an adoption window. We strongly recommend bookmarking this page and reviewing it regularly to remain informed of relevant technical updates. 
 
As part of the platform’s continuous evolution, SAP Commerce Cloud now aligns with Java 21 and Spring 6, establishing a modern, secure, and high‑performance technology foundation. To remain on a supported update and avoid build failures, SAP advises adopting the Framework Update as soon as possible, as the Java 17 releases reached end of support coverage at end of June 2026. At the very latest, please ensure the update is completed by August 31, 2026, after which new builds targeting Java 17 will be disallowed. 
 
Please note that the update removes deprecated extensions and modules and could necessitate code refactoring or configuration updates.
 
To provide additional transition time and flexibility for planning and execution, Solr 9.8 will remain available until August 31, 2026.
 
This quarter, the Framework Update should have the highest priority in your planning strategy. Carefully read the information below:
  • Framework Update Overview (JDK 21, Spring 6)
  • Dependencies of Framework Update:
    • OAuth replacement - The existing OAuth capability has been rebuilt on current Spring libraries. Note that this has an impact on frontend implementations of authorization. See More
    • Drools Update - The previous Drools version 8.44.0. Final is incompatible with Spring 6, as the Java EE API has been upgraded to the Jakarta EE API in Spring 6. 
    • 3rd Party Libraries - Custom implementations may include 3rd party libraries that are not part of the SAP Commerce Cloud out-of-the-box product. These dependencies must likewise be identified, reviewed, and validated to ensure compatibility.
    • Integrations - An up-to-date inventory of all Independent Software Vendor (ISV) and partner integrations connected to or complementing SAP Commerce Cloud implementations should be maintained, such as payment providers, tax solutions, and other direct integrations, with proactive coordination across implementation partners and software vendors to verify ongoing compatibility.
  • Deprecation of Extensions with the Framework Update:
    • SAP has removed several extensions from Commerce Cloud and from the Integration Extension Pack due to incompatibilities with the newest versions of Java and the Spring framework. Replacement solutions already exist for each use case and are documented on the SAP Help Portal. These deprecated extensions are highlighted on the Notification of Key Technical Updates SAP Help page. Use the "Production Version" filter for September 2025 for a complete overview review the following:

      Deletion of Deprecated Cockpit Extensions
      Deletion of Deprecated amazoncloud Extension
      Deletion of Deprecated Sales Order Simulation Extensions
      Deletion of Deprecated Customer Order Sourcing Extensions
      Deletion of Synchronous Order Management as well as the SAP For Me Note
 
The removal of the CMS Cockpit and migration paths to SmartEdit are further documented here:
  • Migrating from CMS Cockpit to SmartEdit_Solution Overview.mp4  
  • Getting Started with SmartEdit 
  • SmartEdit Feature Parity
  • Technical Overview of SmartEdit
For more information on the Framework Update, refer to the following documentation:
  • Framework Update – Detailed Overview
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Framework Update FAQ
  • SAP Help Framework Update 
  • List of supported SAP Commerce Cloud releases
For instructions on enabling new features and functionality, see: 
  • Enabling SAP Commerce Cloud 
  • Feature Flags in the Composable Storefront documentation

SmartEdit

Angular has been upgraded from version 19 to 21 as part of the June 2026 update release.

Library Changes

To improve security and reduce compliance issues, Charon has been deprecated in the June 2026 update and replaced with industry-standard, open-source libraries: Spring WebClient and RestClient.

Charon is planned to be removed from SAP Commerce Cloud in Q4 2026. See More

2 – Stay Current on Composable Storefront and Compatible with its Dependencies

Composable Storefront

SAP Commerce Cloud ensures that all new releases of Composable Storefront are fully tested and compatible with the current 2211 Commerce Cloud releases. Please note that older backend updates are not supported. To avoid issues, review the list of supported Composable Storefront releases below and confirm you are running an update that SAP supports.
 
To adopt the Framework Update, you will need to upgrade to the latest Composable Storefront release. For added flexibility, SAP Commerce Cloud will continue to support Composable Storefront on the current framework (JDK 17) until August 31, 2026. After this date:
  • Any Composable Storefront version earlier than a valid 221121.x update, will no longer be eligible to go live on the Framework Update.
  • New builds will be blocked for unsupported versions and update releases.
Also, since the March 2026 update of Composable Storefront, the new authorizationCodeFlowByDefault authentication flow has been enabled by default. The option to disable this authentication flow is planned for removal in the September 2027 update of Composable Storefront. See More
 
To maintain build and deployment capability, please upgrade to a current update of 221121.x before August 31, 2026. See More

Node.js 20

Additionally, building Composable Storefront and SSR hosting is only supported with the latest supported versions of Node.js, provided by the Cloud Portal. Note that support ended on January 1, 2026 for Node.js 20. Please review the dependencies matrix below and migrate to Node.js 22 as soon as possible, if you have not yet done so, to ensure compatibility and continued support.

Angular

As part of our yearly update cycle, Angular has been upgraded from Angular 19 to 21 on February 20, 2026. This framework update enables standalone components, while keeping Modules for backwards compatibility. Plan ahead to align your projects with this update. See More
 
For more information, refer to the following documentation:
  • List of supported Composable Storefront releases; includes supported backends 
  • Angular and Node.js dependencies matrix

3 – Review Validity and Compatibility of all Features, Services and Updates  

The ecosystem surrounding your SAP Commerce Cloud installation includes many more components. You must maintain these components to stay compatible with the SAP Commerce Cloud releases. SAP has made significant efforts to align the releases of components, such as Composable Storefront.

Solr

An important component of Commerce Cloud is Solr and requires independent lifecycle management. While SAP Commerce Cloud follows a continuous innovation release model in which monthly updates are current for a minimum of 6 months from their release date, Solr updates are not fully aligned with Commerce Cloud update releases but follow its own version validity and maintenance lifecycle. As a result, Solr upgrades require proactive monitoring as well to remain supported. Be sure to plan your activities according to the Solr Version Validity Policy and update your installation of Solr before the expiration date.
 
Current supported versions of Solr are v9.8 and v9.10; however, please note that customers running v9.8 must update to v9.10 before August 31, 2026. 
 
For more information, refer to the following documentation:
  • Minor Solr Version Validity
  • Upgrading to Solr 9.x
SAP offers the Search Service, a fully managed SaaS solution that removes the need for customer‑driven Solr upgrades while providing the same core search capabilities and enabling AI‑driven enhancements through Intelligent Selling Services.

Deletion of Deprecated Password Hashing Algorithms

The deprecated password hashing algorithms are deleted. See More

Going Headless

In alignment with the SAP Commerce Cloud mission to provide a modern, headless commerce experience with easier upgrades of back-end functionality, we invite you to bookmark and explore the Going Headless presentation for an overview of modern storefront architecture and the benefits it can offer beyond the Accelerator UIs.
 
To give you the flexibility to plan your transformation on your terms, the Accelerator UI templates will no longer be tied to fixed de-release dates. Customers can continue to use and deploy existing Accelerator-based storefronts and will continue to receive product support. However, no new features or innovations will be delivered on Accelerator UI templates. We encourage customers to modernize at their own pace in alignment with their business priorities and budget cycles.

Deprecated AddOn Based OCC V2 Extensions

In alignment with the SAP Commerce Cloud mission to provide a modern, headless commerce experience with easier upgrades of back-end functionality, deprecated AddOn-based OCC v2 extensions will be removed and the end of mainstream maintenance ends in Q3 2027. See More
 

What's New?

Details about what’s available now and what you can expect in upcoming releases can be found here:
 
For Commerce Cloud 2211:
  • About SAP Commerce Cloud Release 2211
  • What’s New in SAP Commerce Cloud
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Product Road Map
  • Notification of Key Technical Updates
For Composable Storefront:
  • About Composable Storefront
  • What’s New in Composable Storefront
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Product Road Map

entry.title: Two SAP Signavio Process Governance Challenges We Solved in a Real Customer Project
entry.content:
Last week, I sat together with an SAP partner to discuss two concrete implementation questions in an ongoing customer project around SAP Signavio Process Governance.

In our session, we discussed two very practical requirements from a live implementation. Because both topics come directly from project reality, I want to share them here, explain the background, and describe how we solved them.

At its core, SAP Signavio Process Governance is a cloud-based workflow solution for governing processes, risks, and controls. It supports features such as workflow triggers, human tasks and forms, access control, email-based interactions, and approval workflows for process models.

 

Use Case 1: Sending feedback/ a message to the model creator without having enough SAP Signavio Process Governance licenses

The first practical challenge in this customer scenario was the following:

The customer wanted to send feedback from the workflow back to the person who created the BPMN model. At first glance, this sounds straightforward. However, the issue was that not every model creator had a dedicated SAP Signavio Process Governance user or license.

This becomes relevant because workflows in SAP Signavio Process Governance can only automatically resolve personal user attributes such as first name, last name, and email address if the workflow starter exists as a user in the Governance organization. If that is not the case, the system cannot reliably identify the person as an individual recipient for notifications or follow-up emails.

In practice, this creates a common challenge: the business wants to send personalized workflow feedback to the model creator, but it does not want/can not assign a Process Governance license to every potential user.

In this project, we discussed two possible solution approaches.

Option 1: Assign SAP Signavio Process Governance users to the relevant starters

The cleanest and most robust solution is to ensure that the people who start the workflow are also maintained as users in SAP Signavio Process Governance.

This means that the workflow can directly use the standard creator-related fields, such as the case creator’s email address, first name, and last name. As a result, feedback emails can be sent automatically to the correct person without any workaround logic.

This is clearly the best-practice approach, because it gives the customer a stable, system-supported setup and also creates the right foundation for future governance scenarios such as escalations, task assignments, reminders, and auditability.

The downside is obvious: this approach requires enough SAP Signavio Process Governance licenses for the users who are expected to start these workflows.

Option 2: Use a workaround by capturing the requester details in the workflow

If you can not want to provide additional Process Governance licenses, there is still an alternative.

Instead of relying on the system’s case creator information, the workflow can capture the necessary requester information manually through the start form or an early user task. For example, the workflow can include mandatory fields such as:

  • Requestor name
  • Requestor email address

These values are then stored as workflow variables and used later in the process for email notifications.

With this approach, the workflow no longer depends on the person being a licensed Governance user. The email can still be sent from the workflow, but based on the manually entered requester data instead of the system-resolved case creator profile.

This workaround is especially useful in scenarios where many users may trigger approvals, but only a smaller group should hold actual Process Governance licenses.

The trade-off is that this approach is more manual and therefore more error-prone. Users may enter the wrong email address, make spelling mistakes, or provide inconsistent information. Still, for many customer setups, this is a very pragmatic and effective solution.

The process before the change

The process after the change we only needed to add one task and change one existing task.

The normal approval process was enriched with two more fields: 

The added task (send Mail to the requestor/process modeler) 

Key takeaway

So, the core of the first use case is this:

If you want to send workflow feedback to the creator of a BPMN model, but you do not have enough SAP Signavio Process Governance users or licenses for every workflow starter, you have two options:

  • Best practice: license the relevant starters as Governance users
  • Workaround: capture the requester’s email and name manually in the workflow and use these values for notifications

Both approaches work. The right choice depends on whether the customer prioritizes a clean, fully system-driven setup or a leaner licensing model with some manual input.

Use Case 2: Sending rejection feedback or details from the process automatically to the requester

The second practical challenge in this scenario was closely related to the first one.

The customer wanted to ensure that the person who submitted the request also receives direct feedback if the process model is rejected. More specifically, if an approver rejects the BPMN model, the requester should automatically be informed about the reason for the rejection.

This is an important requirement in real governance scenarios. A simple rejection status is often not enough. If model creators or requesters do not understand why a model was rejected, the process quickly becomes inefficient. The request is sent back, but the next revision may still not address the real issue. As a result, approval cycles become longer and more frustrating for everyone involved.

The good news is that this requirement can typically be implemented in SAP Signavio Process Governance without custom development.

The functional idea

The basic design is simple:

When an approver rejects the request, they do not only choose “Rejected”, but also enter a reason for the rejection in a dedicated form field, for example:

  • Rejection reason
  • Feedback to requestor
  • Comment on rejection

This information is then stored as part of the workflow data.

After that, the workflow follows the rejection path and triggers an automatic email task. In this email, the process can include:

  • the rejection status
  • the rejection reason entered by the approver
  • optionally, additional guidance on what needs to be changed before resubmission

This way, the requester receives immediate and structured feedback directly from the workflow.

Why this matters

This may sound like a small detail, but from a business perspective it makes a major difference.

Instead of just informing the requester that the submission was rejected, the workflow closes the communication gap and provides actionable feedback. This improves transparency, reduces unnecessary back-and-forth, and helps the requester revise the BPMN model more efficiently.

In other words, the workflow does not just control approval. It also supports continuous improvement.

Dependency on recipient information

The only important prerequisite is that the workflow must know where to send the email.

This connects directly back to the first use case.

There are again two possible setups:

Option 1: The requester exists as a Governance user

If the requester or workflow starter is maintained as a user in SAP Signavio Process Governance, the workflow can use the standard creator-related user information and send the email directly to that person.

This is the cleanest setup because recipient information is resolved automatically by the system. 

(Spoiler Option 1 is the easiest one and the one I would recommend, because you have with option 2 the same issues like in use case no. 1) 

Option 2: The requester data was captured manually in the workflow

If the requester/receiver  is not a licensed Governance user, the workflow can still send the feedback email, provided that the requester’s email address was captured earlier in the process through a form field.

In that case, the rejection email is not sent to the technical case creator profile, but to the manually maintained requester email stored in the workflow.

This means that the second use case can also be solved without assigning additional Governance licenses, as long as the requester information is collected explicitly at the beginning of the workflow.

The result: 

I reuse the attributes I created in Use Case No.1

The workflow in usage.

In the new task I added the attributes from use case no 1. 

I added also attributes in the text field so the mail has the written feedback from the use case no. 1 simply use "#" and add the field of your choice. 

The mail I got. You see If I have a mistake in the mail--> I don't get the mail or maybe some other errors are in the mail

 

Key takeaway

The core of the second use case is this:

If a submitted BPMN model is rejected, the requester should not only see the rejection result, but also receive the actual feedback explaining the decision.

This can be implemented in SAP Signavio Process Governance by:

  1. adding a rejection comment field in the approval step
  2. storing that feedback in the workflow
  3. sending it automatically in a follow-up email task to the requester

So the process becomes much more than a simple approve-or-reject mechanism. It becomes a structured governance workflow that actively supports communication and better rework cycles.

Final thoughts

To be honest, I believe SAP Signavio Process Governance is a underestimated modul in the SAP Signavio portfolio. Many people primarily associate SAP Signavio with modeling, mining, and analysis and SPG with the approval workflow for BPMN models, but it can do way more e.g. to operationalizing governance, approvals, responsibilities, and workflow-based control, Process Governance is where things become actionable.

For me, this was a reminder that SAP Signavio Process Governance is more than just a technical add-on for approvals.

It is the module that turns governance concepts into executable workflows. It connects process modeling (or other topics) with real accountability, real communication, and real operational follow-through.

And that is exactly why it deserves more attention.

If you are currently working on similar requirements around process approvals, requester handling, or workflow notifications in SAP Signavio Process Governance, these two design patterns are worth keeping in your toolbox.

Best Regards

Jonas Germann 

SAP BTM Solution Advisor

entry.title: SAP GRC Process Control Job Scheduling Issues – SAP Notes & Keywords Guide
entry.content:
Automated Control Monitoring (ACM) jobs in SAP GRC Process Control often fail due to hidden technical, authorization, or configuration issues.

Instead of generic troubleshooting, this blog provides a keyword-driven SAP Notes mapping, allowing you to:

  • Search error text directly
  • Identify exact SAP Note
  • Fix issues faster

2. SAP Notes + Exact Keywords + Symptoms Mapping

🔴 A. Job Not Triggering / Scheduling Issues

SAP Note Encrypted (Search Terms) Symptoms Reference
3593052failed to create issue, reschedule job step failed, mc_failed_create_issue, issue creation error, job step retry failure, scheduler reschedule errorJob remains in Scheduled / not executed
3328050event-based monitoring issue not created, event scenario failure, no issue generated, monitoring job success but no issue, trigger failureJob not picked by scheduler
3312097integration evidence missing, MCP CCM integration issue, case integration incomplete, missing attachment/evidence, job step incompleteJob skipped / incomplete
3248870SQL deadlock, DB lock situation, data rollback, update termination, job dump during execution, LUW rollbackSilent failure / no execution
2919879wrong start time, mismatch with SM37, scheduling error, incorrect job start timestampJob missing / incorrect scheduling
2911406filter not applied, incorrect results, BR filter issueJob condition / logic issue

🔴 B. Job Execution Failures / Dumps

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
3426819CCM job error, module mc_failed_job_grp_ci_al_done, case integration failure, job log module error, CI integration issueDump during execution
3701175field offset too large dump, runtime error, internal table overflow, memory structure issue, job dumpRuntime dump
3695307runtime error during notification, inconsistent issue data, job failure during notification, monitoring inconsistencyJob stops midway
3694020timeframe truncated, incorrect period, job monitor shows wrong dates, incomplete timeframe displayUnexpected termination
3693482FAIL link short dump, UI action dump, navigation error, runtime dump on clickUI/runtime dump
3565749downtime scheduling issue, job executes immediately, incorrect scheduling after downtime, reschedule anomalyExecution anomaly
3525337BRF+ update not reflected, rule changes ignored, outdated rule execution, inconsistency in rule processingLogic failure
3446826notification missing, no email alert, event trigger failureNotification failure
2968656dependent job not triggered, chain job failure, workflow breakJob cancelled
3381181short dump MESSAGE_TYPE_X_TEXT, CL_GRPC_TESTLOG_API dump, case does not exist, ASSERT failure, runtime termination, job crashDump in execution

🔴 C. Data Fetch / Mapping Issues

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
3615018error in basic case API, job log error, CCM scheduling failure, configurable scenario dump, case API failure, job execution stopsMissing fields
3612749SOD failure, risk analysis failed, SOD job not executing, dependency failureIncorrect data
3572507duplicate email notifications, multiple alerts for same job, repeated failure emailsData inconsistency
3555614SOD results missing, blank job monitor, no data shown, evaluation missingBlank fields
3553362backend dump, job step runtime error, short dump during execution, technical dumpPartial data
3521866slow performance, job monitor lag, long execution time, UI delay, backend performance issueMissing attributes / slow fetch
3495734job logs missing, no logs in SLG1, queue logs not available, debugging issueData/log mismatch

🔴 D. Incorrect Results / Report Issues

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
2582160issue creation failure, no issue created, job error during issue creation, backend issue creation dumpOutput mismatch
2401507reschedule failed job steps not working, retry job step fails, scheduler limitation, job step stuckInconsistent results
2573820type BR does not exist, object not found, job execution failure due to missing BRMapping issue
2388122job step failed but deficiencies created, inconsistent result, partial failureMissing entries
2643942cancel job error, termination failure, scheduler cancel issueDisplay issue

🔴 E. Authorization Issues

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
3370349error during obsoleting job step, cannot obsolete job step, job step status failure, obsolete action dumpAuthorization-related failure
2064944UTC vs system time mismatch, wrong timezone display, job monitor time incorrect, SM37 mismatchExecution inconsistency
2010831navigation dump, job step link crash, UI dumpAccess/UI issue

🔴 F. Master Data / Configuration Issues

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
2846218job status not updated, status mismatch frontend/backend, job shows completed but actually error, inconsistent statusControls not picked
1909373wrong execution time, job monitor time mismatch, incorrect duration, scheduler vs monitor mismatchOrg/config issue
2129930job stuck in error status, automated job failure, job not completing, persistent error stateMissing assignments
1954054cancel job failure, event job cannot be stopped, scheduler issueConfig issue
2199574reschedule issue, job step retry not working, scheduler failureInvalid config

🔴 G. Performance / Miscellaneous

SAP Note Exact Keywords Symptoms
3630375internal server error, overwrite protected field, data source application error, HTTP 500 error, backend overwrite dumpPerformance / backend issue

3. How to Use This Effectively

  • Copy error text from:
    • Job log (SM37)
    • Dump (ST22)
    • Application log (SLG1)
  • Search using exact keywords above
  • Identify matching SAP Note
  • Implement fix via SNOTE

4. Real Case Mapping (Your Scenario)

Issue:
Risk category fields not coming

Keywords:

  • error in basic case API
  • case API failure
  • job execution stops

SAP Note: 3615018

5. Conclusion

Using SAP Notes + exact keywords + symptoms mapping provides a fast, reliable way to troubleshoot ACM job scheduling and execution issues in SAP GRC Process Control.

Hint:
#SAPGRC #ProcessControl #ACM #SAPNotes #ErrorHandling
@SAPSupport @GRCExperts @ABAPDevelopers

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Leverage Mexico’s IMMEX program with SAP Global Trade Services and Origen Tech
entry.content:
In a world where fluctuations in tariffs and trade tensions between nations can impact the terms and conditions of international trade, being able to optimize costs by claiming duty relief from customs duties or applying for refunds on already paid ones, can significantly improve a company’s competitiveness and strengthen its market presence.

In some geographies, customs authorities allow companies to leverage special procedures providing some financial leeway for import and export processing, enabling cross-border manufacturing processes, and ultimately facilitating global supply chains.

When companies transfer products to special customs procedures, they may be able to reduce or avoid customs duties altogether or at least delay them until the goods in question enter the country or customs union of destination.

In exchange, however, customs authorities demand special diligence. Which is why they require authorizations for each of these customs procedures. In addition, authorities also demand special monitoring of the processes, including mandatory inventory management for these customs procedures.

One of the countries with such a program is Mexico.

 

Industria Manufacturera, Maquiladora y de Servicios de Exportación (IMMEX) program

 

Originally introduced as a special government program in 1965 to promote manufacturing investment on the United States-Mexican border, create employment opportunities, improve Mexico’s trade balance and drive technological development, the special customs procedure program in Mexico has continued to expand in usage when the country became a major manufacturing hub for companies looking to locate assembly operations outside the United States but still benefit from the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) – replaced in 2020 by the United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA).

A new version of the program was launched in 2006 – the Industria Manufacturera, Maquiladora y de Servicios de Exportación (IMMEX) program, and “allows foreign manufacturers to import raw materials and components into Mexico, tax- and duty-free, under the condition that 100% of all finished goods will be exported out of Mexico within a government-mandated time frame” (North American Production Sharing). As of December 2025, the IMMEX program totalled 6,512 sites (plants and warehouses) and over 3 million jobs, thus making it not only a duty optimization program, but also a compelling operational strategy supporting supply chain resilience in North America. This includes a layer of protection since the USMCA defines a legal trade framework across North America.

As mentioned in the introduction, however, certain obligations must be met to be able to benefit from such a program.

In addition to eligibility criteria, for trade compliance specifically, there are 2 major obligations under IMMEX: Annex 24 and Annex 30. Together, they require organizations to:

  • Maintain an automated inventory control tracking temporarily imported goods
  • Ensure that goods are exported within the authorized import time frames

If these are not respected, the SAT (Servicio de Administración Tributaria) might require the company to pay applicable taxes, and could also impose fines and penalties that could even compromise a company’s IMMEX certification.

As you may already know, SAP Global Trade Service, edition for HANA supports inventory-managed special customs procedures in the European Union, but also in the United States Free Trade Zones and in China’s processing trade regime. But IMMEX was not yet supported.

Working with Origen Tech, this gap has now been covered and organizations can extend their SAP Global Trade Service solution with Origen Tech’s localization offering for Mexico to optimize import operations, pre-process import declarations, gain access to customs brokers and logistics operators, and enter advance payments and additional expenses in SAP Global Trade Services, with direct integration with the purchasing and accounting system in ERP (S/4 HANA or SAP ECC).

 

Who is Origen Tech

 

Founded in 2012, Origen Tech is a US-based SAP Gold Partner with expertise in SAP consulting, products and solution development focused on helping companies with their compliance and localization needs in the United States, Brazil  & Latin America. With headquarters located in Miami, Origen Tech also has presence in the main economies in Latin America.

A long-time partner for SAP Global Trade Services, but also SAP Document Reporting and Compliance, Origen Tech delivers localized country offerings and has built SAP partner extensions using the technology e-documents framework to those countries that are not on the planned SAP roadmap to enable real time e-invoicing and statutory reporting worldwide. This includes IMMEX in Mexico.

 

Why did SAP decide to partner in this area?

 

While SAP focuses on delivering core SAP Global Trade Services capabilities globally and full localization for chosen countries, we collaborate with trusted partners to extend the solution and offer customers localized functionalities tailored to their specific regional needs. 

When doing so, we focus on 5 main areas:

  • Time to market: so that the offering can be delivered rapidly when customers need it, and provide flexibility when regulators enforce new rules
  • Industry-specific: when requirements are global or mandatory for all companies, they will of course be included in the roadmap for the standard product but, for industry or geographic specifics, focused partners are more agile and closer to the need
  • Assurance: these extensions must follow SAP’s quality mandates to protect customers and maintain trust
  • Teamwork: since these enhancements are the result of a collaboration between 3 parties – the customer at the center, the partner who orchestrates and delivers the innovation, and SAP supporting the partner on technical aspects
  • State of the art: these enhancements natively integrate into SAP Global Trade Services since they are built on the same technology

 

What is the SAP Global Trade Services Mexico localization from Origen Tech?

 

By implementing Origen Tech's Mexico localization solution, businesses gain the ability to streamline and automate their cross-border supply chain processes using SAP Global Trade Services - enhancing global trade management, reducing costs, mitigating risks, and expediting customs clearance. Hence enabling companies to leverage the IMMEX program allowing for tax-free imports for re-exported products.

Dedicated SAP Global Trade Services add-on features delivered with this localization include:

 

o   Material classification under Mexico’s Commercial Identification Number (NICO) scheme

o   Data stage integration to run analytical reports, and compare official custom forms (pedimentos) against system generated declarations in SAP Global Trade Services, automatically identifying discrepancies

o   Total cost of duty imports determination, including planned and unplanned costs

o   Facilitated communication with customs brokers, including the electronic file (expediente electrónico)

 

 Additionally, this localization allows direct integration with the Mexican authorities’ foreign trade receipt system (Ventanilla Única de Comercio Exterior Mexicana, or VUCEM) to obtain the final import documentation.

 

What benefits can organizations – and their trade compliance department, expect?

 

If helping companies optimize customs duties by avoiding “worst case” determination and strengthen their market presence by improving their product competitiveness is of course the ultimate benefit, to achieve this objective companies must ensure compliance with the mandatory requirements. In this aspect, this localization helps trade compliance departments:

 

Manage and apply trade preferences and agreements

 

Determine eligibility for preferential tariff rates based on trade agreements

 

Automate calculations of applicable preferences during import/export

 

Facilitate compliance with trade preference regulations for cost optimization

 

Interested in learning more?

 

Then feel free to view the recording of the live co-innovation session between Origen Tech and SAP on the theme SAP Global Trade Services Localisation for Mexico and Brazil delivered at the SAP for Global Trade and Tax Conference organized by TAC Insights in Rome in November 2025.

And, if you are looking for more details on IMMEX and how Origen Tech can extend your SAP Global Trade Service edition for HANA to meet the specific custom requirements, then have a look at the dedicated page: IMMEX program for Mexico

entry.title: Customizing GRC NWBC screens globally without PFCG role update
entry.content:
Challenge: Fragmented GRC NWBC appearance and work center structure for different user groups in organisation leading to inconsistent UI layouts when accessing GRC functionality through NWBC, leading to reduced productivity and increased training costs. A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be established.

 

Implementation Approach: A uniform NWBC Landing page need to be configured which is applicable across all users accessing NWBC launch screen without changing roles.

In this blog, the detailed steps for configuring NWBC screens in highlighted.

Benefits

  • Eliminates Need for Technical ABAP Changes

Many minor UI adjustments, such as showing/hiding: Hyperlinks, Menu sections, Application nodes can be handled via customizing mode without involving developers

  • Helps Maintain Audit & Compliance Standards

Since NWBC is used by auditors, risk teams, and provisioning teams, controlling UI visibility helps ensure Least privilege navigation, No exposure of admin tools to business users, Clear segmentation of duties within the UI.

 

Environment: GRC Access Control 12.0

 

To achieve Centralized Control of UI Structure, GRC Admins can change what appears on NWBC for all users, from a single configuration point. No need to adjust individual roles repeatedly.

 In NWBC landing page, Right click -> option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’.

 

Segregation of Duties approach to prevent unintended customization:

Authorization object S_WDR_ADM controls the access to option ‘Call Application in customizing mode’ through which elements like visibility of each hyperlink/section can be globally changed for all users.

  • GRC Admins (Super user) or User designated to perform NWBC UI changes can be assigned with Authorization object S_WDR_ADM with full access in the PFCG role
  • For all other users, restrict Authorization object S_WDR_ADM to display only or disable it in the PFCG role.

 

Steps:

NOTE:  Exercise caution in applying these changes as it will be applicable to all users accessing NWBC irrespective of role assignment.

For Example - If there is a requirement that hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ should not be visible to any users who uses NWBC, then we can follow the below steps

1. Right Click on hyperlink ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ -> Call Application in Customizing mode

2. Choose the Transport Request

3. Right click -> Call Application in Classic Customizing Mode

4. Right click -> Settings for Current Configuration

5. Set the Visibility to Invisible. 

6. After the changes are applied, ‘Work Inbox – Simplified’ hyperlink is not visible. You can always switch it back to default by following the same previous steps

 

You can follow the same steps to customise other NWBC screen hyperlinks or buttons.

 

Conclusion:

You have now successfully customised the GRC NWBC screen to display only required hyperlinks for all users.

entry.title: Configuring custom GRC NWBC Launch Screens restricted via PFCG roles
entry.content:
Challenge: Standard SAP GRC roles for NetWeaver Business Client (NWBC) grant excessive access privileges that exceed users' actual job requirements. This over-provisioning creates security risks and poor user experience, as users encounter numerous irrelevant hyperlinks and folders they don't need for their daily tasks.

Implementation Approach: The solution leverages transaction LPD_CUST (Configure Launchpad for Menus) to design custom NWBC landing pages. This involves copying standard Webdynpro application configuration, component configuration and UIBB component in which LPD_CUST role is linked.

In this blog, the detailed steps for configuring LPD_CUST, Web Dynpro Configuration and PFCG mapping is highlighted.

Environment: GRC Access Control 12.0

Launchpad Flow chart:

 

Section 1: LPD_CUST launchpad customisation

In Tcode LPD_CUST, we need to define the customization that is required for the workset, here we have considered GRACHOME (GRC Access Control Home Workset). It is recommended to take a copy of this workset to custom naming convention to preserve standard configuration.

Select line item GRACHOME, Click on copy.

 

 

Enter your custom values and namespace.

 

 

In ZGRACHOME, You can make all the required customisations. Here, for simplicity, I have modified the folder name to ‘Work Inbox - CUSTOM’.

Potential Use case 1: For Approvers users, you can keep only ‘Work Inbox’, Delete ‘Work Inbox -  Simplified’ to make it more clear to users as to which one to use.

Potential Use case 2: In Application help, You can place company’s intranet URL link that points to a guide on how to review and approve requests. For this, you can create ‘New Application’ , select application type ‘URL’ and add the required URL.

 

You can follow the same steps to customize Setup, Access Management, Reports and Analytics Webdynpro Application

 

Section 2: NWBC Webdynpro customisation

In tcode PFCG, copy standard role SAP_GRAC_NWBC to custom role Z_TEST_NWBC,

Tcode PFCG -> Z_TEST_NWBC

Here, we are customizing the ‘My Home’ Webdynpro application GRFN_SERVICE_MAP.

Right click on ‘My Home’ Webdynpro application -> Details

We see the standard Configuration: GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME. We want to customize this Application Configuration to point our desired ‘My Home’ Layout in NWBC which we configured in LPD_CUST.

Click on ‘Application Configuration’ button.

 

This will launch a web URL which takes you to application configuration.

 

 

From here on, the logic is that we copy the Application Configuration, Component Configuration, UIBB Configuration and In UIBB configuration we point it to Role /instance of LPD_CUST. Refer to the flowchart.

Step 1: Create Copy of Application Configuration.

 
Click on Other Functions -> New Window -> Copy

 

 

 

Provide custom configuration ID and description

 

Select the relevant package, here I chose $TMP (Non-transportable), Click OK.

 

 

Click Change

 

We enter into the custom application configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME

Click on hyper link GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

You will reach Component Configuration screen as below

 

Step 2: Create copy of component configuration

Click on Other Functions -> New Window -> Copy

 

Provide the custom configuration ID and description

 

Enter the desired package

 

Click Change

 

Click on Edit -> Cancel to get in Display mode, Click on hyperlink ‘GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME’

 

Step 3: Create copy of UIBB configuration

In this window, Click on Other Functions -> New Window

Click Copy

 

Provide the custom configuration ID and description

 

Click Change.

In this window, we need to map ZGRACHOME in Role field (earlier created in LPD_CUST).

Change it to ZGRACHOME. All the customizations done in ZGRACHOME can be previewed. Notice that we had changed folder ‘Work Inbox – CUSTOM’ in this scenario.

Click Save.

Select the TR and Click OK

 

Section 3: PFCG Role Configuration mapping

Step 1: In tcode PFCG, Role Z_TEST_NWBC

Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Details -> Change configuration to Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME

Click Save

 

Step 2: Again, Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Details -> Click Application Configuration.

 

This will open up URL in web browser, we see that we are in custom application configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_AC_LPD_HOME, we see standard component configuration is mapped, we will need to change to custom component configuration Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

Click on Edit -> Assign Configuration Name

Assign the custom component configuration ID Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

Click Save.

 

Step 3: Click the hyperlink Z1GRAC_FPM_CC_LPD_HOME

 

This will open up Custom component configuration, we see that standard UIBB is mapped, but we need to change to custom UIBB Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

 

Click on F4 button on GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME, change it to Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

Click Save

 

Step 4: Click on Edit -> Cancel. We are in display mode. Click on hyperlink Z1GRAC_FPM_UIBB_LPD_HOME

 

You will find all the elements mapped as per the flowchart

 

Assign the PFCG role Z_TEST_NWBC to user and test the changes. Or you can Right click on ‘My Home’ -> Execute.

 

The customisation done in LPD_CUST reflects. 

NOTE: If you assign standard role SAP_GRAC_NWBC and custom role Z_TEST_NWBC together to a user, this won’t work as intended since the system is confused whether to pick up the standard application configuration or the custom application configuration defined in the Webdynpro application GRFN_SERVICE_MAP. The intent of creating the custom role is to assign that specific custom role Z_TEST_NWBC to satisfy all NWBC access requirements for the user.

You can follow the same steps to customize Setup, Access Management, Reports and Analytics Webdynpro Application.

 

Conclusion:

You have now successfully created a custom PFCG role with Web Dynpro application configuration pointing to Custom NWBC screen.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Watch List Screening – Delta Screening & Streamlined Investigation
entry.content:
With advance in technology, also comes increased expectations from regulatory bodies for companies to rapidly adapt to changing circumstances. For instance, when it comes to sanctioned party screening, authorities now prioritize real-time compliance, contextual analysis and enhanced due diligence processes over ad-hoc list-matching.

To continuously support organizations in this area, multiple enhancements have once again been delivered to SAP Watch List Screening.
