In this short blog, I have selected a few, but you will also find more details in the dedicated section of the SAP Help Portal: What’s New in SAP Watch List Screening

 

Delta Screening

 

If there is no predetermined timetable for sanction lists update but rather names and entities being added or removed by authorities as necessary and appropriate, one thing is sure: this is quite frequent with some lists being modified as regularly as daily. Not only to add or remove records, but to include additional details, aliases, updated addresses, etc.

And, if experts assess that the percentage of these updates is limited (some claim less than 1% of total records are updated daily), this still means a great volume of changes that organizations need to remain compliant with.

To do so, compliance departments can opt for 2 options:

  • Systematic full rescreening of all records and entire sanction list set => this is the “bullet proof” approach – or at least as close as it gets of course, but equates in practice to a new full screening at every run so it is heavy and not efficient for ongoing monitoring
  • Delta screening with targeted compliance measures that re-check only newly added or updated watch list records for potential sanction hits => with this approach, Compliance Officers benefit from faster, more focused checks, saving time and ensuring that newly added or updated data is promptly screened without missing potential risks

Full screening was already available in SAP Watch List Screening since the very inception of this service where documents were re-checked upon changes and further processing and business partners had an own scheduling report for full re-screenings.

Delta Screening has been added in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Edition 2602 and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise release 2025 FPS2. With Delta Screening, the changes in watch lists are monitored and trigger a screening re-run on customer information - request status, business partners, and documents.

Allow me to illustrate this with a simple example from the SAP Help Portal section for this enhancement:

A business partner is screened on creation, resulting in no hits. Therefore, the business partner is released. Some days later, a new watch list version is uploaded containing new watch list entries. SAP Watch List Screening now automatically screens the business partner against these new watch list entries and finds a hit and blocks the business partner. The next postprocessing run fetches this update and blocks the business partner in the system accordingly. In doing so, the screening status of the business partner (as well as the documents) stays up-to-date with watch list changes, ensuring consistency and compliance.

With delta screening, companies can:

 

 

Audit Trail

 

Full auditability of sanction party list screenings is often a legal requirement within sanction regulations. For instance, under 31 CFR Part 501 - Records and recordkeeping requirements, the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) mandates that organizations “keep a full and accurate record of each such transaction engaged in”.

Complete logs of who performed the screening, what was screened, and when it took place help businesses accomplish just that: demonstrate compliance, manage liability, and foster trust by showing that processes are transparent and defensible.

To support this requirement, an new Fiori app has been released: Display Audit Trail.

With this app organizations – or their auditors – can display an audit trail for their screening requests and navigate to individual screening requests for further investigation:

 

 

Display Watch Lists

 

Following on the topic of audit, you may recall that we previously mentioned in a What’s new in SAP Watch List Screening blog the availability of Custom Watch Lists that organizations can upload in addition to the provided list.

Whether it be for predefined provided lists or custom lists, auditors, but also compliance managers and screening specialists have asked for the ability to access the details of the lists:

 

Auditors

To audit and verify that the correct watch lists are in use

 

Compliance managers

To assess the completeness and coverage of screening content and verify their custom lists post-upload

 

Screening specialists

For support during an investigation to understand why certain names are flagged and pre-emptively analyse watch list contents

 

Here again, to help customers, a new Fiori app has been released: Display Watch Lists

With this app users can display all entries of the watch lists they are using:

 

 

Link back to original watch list source

 

Finally, and to close this blog on investigation by screening specialists, when a hit is generated, these experts need to verify if the associated entity is truly the one listed on the sanction party list. To do so, they often need to refer back to the original documentation – list from OFAC, United Nations, European Union, country specific… and many more! – where all attributes and comments are detailed, to ensure they enforce the right decision.

With the new capability to link back to the original watch list source, this step of the investigation is streamlined and handling time per alert due to manual searching reduced:

What about you, how does your company address these needs of constantly changing sanction lists and complex investigations? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Global Trade Services - Manage Origin of Goods
entry.content:
As an importer or a domestic operator, accurately collecting and documenting the origin of the externally-procured product and batches is of the utmost importance since it determines the duties, possible trade preference options available, and import restrictions that apply to the shipment.

As a matter of fact, the Country and Region of Origin is a mandatory attribute for Customs treatment, duty assessment, and anti-dumping.

In many organizations, the non-preferential country of origin (COO) is a critical data point used in systems like SAP Global Trade Services for duty calculation, compliance checks, and documentation validation. However, this data is typically gathered and maintained in ERP systems by supply chain teams handling purchasing and sales flows.

This creates a fundamental issue: supply chain personnel often confuse country of origin with country of supply (where goods are shipped from). Since they are not the true owners of this data and are primarily focused on operational execution, COO is frequently entered incorrectly or without proper validation—simply as a mandatory field required for processing.

 The impact is significant. Incorrect COO data leads to:

  • Errors in duty calculations & preference determination
  • Inconsistencies in documentation (e.g. delivery notes, invoices)
  • Time-consuming manual checks and corrections

When discrepancies are identified, they are usually corrected within GTS by customs or compliance teams. However, these corrections are not synchronized back to the ERP, resulting in persistent data misalignment between systems.

At its core, the problem is one of misaligned ownership and responsibility. COO is a compliance-critical attribute that should be owned and governed by customs or GTS teams, yet it is currently maintained by supply chain users who lack the context and accountability to manage it accurately.

An effective solution would involve shifting ownership of COO to customs/GTS, along with better system integration to ensure consistency between ERP and GTS, reducing errors, manual rework, and compliance risk.

In this area, a new Manage Country/Region of Origin app has been delivered with SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA 2025 Feature Pack Stack 01 released in April 2026.

Thanks to this new feature, organizations can:

 

Manage the non-preferential country and region of origin for externally procured products and/or batches

 

Rely on high transparency, by identifying the country at the good receipt level

 

Get automatic update of the country and/or region of origin and region of origin in feeder system

 

Integration with other modules from SAP Global Trade Services, edition for HANA such as preference and customs management

It is important to understand the distinction between non-preferential origin and preferential origin of a product.

Non-preferential origin refers to the country where a product is either wholly obtained or entirely produced. It can also be the last country where the product underwent substantial transformation. Minor or minimal processing carried out in another location does not usually change the product’s origin status.

Preferential origin, on the other hand, applies to products that meet specific criteria set out in a particular free trade agreement. These rules determine whether a product qualifies for reduced or zero tariffs under that agreement.

 

How does it work?

 

With the Manage Country/Region of Origin app, you can manage the country and region of origin for your externally procured products directly.

Provided the country (or region) of origin is available in either an import customs declaration, a long-term supplier declaration (LTSD) or an inbound delivery, it can be retrieved automatically and assigned to the product master data, and batch master data if applicable, in the feeder system. This information can still, of course, be manually overwritten should it be required.

The process starts in the feeder system where the good receipt information resides and is transferred to SAP GTS. There, the information relevant for country of origin is consolidated and analysed in the new app Manage Country/Region of origin and determination approach applied on a scenario logic.

 

Scenario 1 – the country of origin can be determined

 

 

If the country or region of origin is documented and the same is true for every good receipt, the product master data is automatically updated in the feeder system.

Illustration in SAP GTS:

The 2 source documents – here Long-Term Supplier Documents – both display “Spain” as country of origin and “Baleares” as region of origin, so origin information can be determined.

Note: a similar approach applies to batch-managed products with stock levels being considered into the determination to define the country of origin for each batch individually. Here, product master data won’t be updated though but can be manually maintained.

 

Scenario 2 – the country of origin can’t be determined

 

In case the origin information for a given product cannot be automatically established, SAP Global Trade Services will raise an alert. The user can then drill-down into the system to review the source data and investigate: is it due to multiple countries from various suppliers for the same product? Is it due to source documents missing? Etc. If the origin can be established, the user can manually maintain in the source information and correct or adjust the final origin.

Illustration in SAP GTS:

 

Want to learn more?

 

If this short summary wasn’t sufficient to satisfy your curiosity – and it most likely is the case, then I would suggest watching the recording of the SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA 2025 FPS01 Release Overview session that was delivered a few weeks ago.

During this webinar, Volker Mohr from SAP GTS Product Management, Carmen Abelein and Torsten Stolz from SAP GTS Engineering, provided an update on the various enhancements delivered in this new version. Focusing on Preference Management, Customs Management, Product Master Data and of course, a deep-dive into Origin of Goods with various scenarios and a live demo.

 

What’s next?

 

As its name suggest, this is only the very first Feature Pack but many more enhancements are planned to come, including Feature Pack 2 planned for end of the year.

To be amongst the first to know what’s next, you have 2 options – which are not exclusive:

 

1. Register to the SAP Roadmap Update for International Trade Management Solutions session planned for June 23, 2026

 

2. Keep an eye on the SAP Road Map Explorer portal for SAP Global Trade Services

 

 

Want to influence the roadmap?

 

If you would like to help shape the future of the solution, then take part in the Continuous Influence session for SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA. 

Voting for the next review cycle is open until end of June 2026.

Improvement requests are prioritized by the community through voting, so cast your votes for proposed enhancements important to you. Review of an Improvement Request by SAP Product Management will happen when the request has reached the minimum threshold of 20 votes or more from customers with a first review cycle planned in Q3 2026.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

 

Thank you, Prashanth Kaja – SAP GTS Customer Advisory for EMEA, for collaborating on this blog.

 

 

Frequently Asked Questions

 

  • What are the pre-requisites for “Origin of goods” integration with SAP S/4HANA

The prerequisites are mentioned in the SAP Note 3710399 - Origin of goods: Introduction of the function for managing the origin of goods

 

  • What versions of feeder systems support this?

This is supported by feeder system SAP S/4HANA 2021 private cloud edition or higher. SAP ECC is not supported.

 

  • Is the field COO now available in the long-term supplier declaration (LTSD) maintenance?

Yes

 

  • Batches on material level vs Batches at plant level, what is supported?

Currently batches at plant level are supported.

 

  • Will aggregation of long-term supplier declarations (LTSD) still be required? And will preference determination pick the origin from this new app?

Preference determination is independent from origin of goods and does not consider or origin. It depends whether the agreement and yes aggregation of long-term supplier declarations (LTSD) is still required for preference determination.

 

  • Does the stock data get synchronized with the origin that has been determined?

It depends on whether it is a batch-managed product or not. For batched managed products a report is available to update the stock, for non-batched managed product, stock is not considered.

 

  • Will the country of origin in the SAP S/4HANA product master be cleared in case the country is not unique?

It is customizable. It is possible to delete or leave it as-is until the user decides to manually intervene.

 

  • How can we maintain multiple origin countries for a single product ID?

This is only possible when batches are activated and used.

entry.title: How to implement Organization Rule to filter out false-positives in SOD Risk Analysis in GRC Access
entry.content:
Organizational Rules in SAP GRC Access Control are specialized filters designed to eliminate false positive segregation of duties (SoD) violations in access risk analysis reports. These rules enable organizations to account for organizational-level restrictions when evaluating access risks, ensuring that conflicts are only reported when they occur within the same organizational boundary (such as company code, cost center, or plant)

Objective:

The primary objective is exception-based reporting to remove false positives that result from organizational-level segregation, where users may have conflicting transactions but are restricted by different organizational values

Scenario / Challenge:

An organization has implemented a shared services model where finance teams are segregated by company code. The current SoD analysis shows false positive conflicts when users have vendor invoice posting rights (FB60) for Company Code 1008 and vendor master data maintenance rights (FK02) for Company Code 1000. While these are conflicting functions, they pose no actual risk since they operate on different organizational entities.

Good Practices for Configuration:

Organizations should implement organizational rules exclusively for exception-based reporting and only after thorough analysis to ensure the situation warrants their use. Rules should not be instituted until the remediation phase of a GRC project, and only after identifying specific organizational rule scenarios.

Critical considerations include:

  • Use organizational rules only when the company has made a conscious decision to segregate via organization levels

  • Avoid using organizational rules for mass organizational level reporting due to performance impact

  • Be cautious not to filter out too much, as it's better to over-report (false positives) than under-report (false negatives) from a control perspective

  • After creating organizational rules, SoD rules must be regenerated using transaction GRAC_GENERATE_RULES

  • Apply the "Consider Org rule" option during risk analysis execution to activate organizational rule filtering

Most companies control data access through role assignment rather than organizational rules, so this functionality should only be enabled for functions requiring specific organizational segregation

Implementation:

Step 1: Identify the specific SoD risk: "Vendor Master Maintenance vs. Process Vendor Invoices"

SOD Setup Diagram:

 

To keep it simple. I have created SOD Risk ‘ZP001_1’ containing two functions ‘ZAP02_1’and ‘ZPR01_1’.

Generate the Risk after creation or modification ( Tcode: GRAC_GENERATE_RULES )

Risk: ZP001_1

 

 

Function: ZAP02_1

 

 

 

Function: ZPR01_1

 

 

Note:

I have added permission which contains BUKRS field as we are considering implementing Organizational Rule for Company Code.

$BUKRS in SAP GRC ruleset permission files is a variable placeholder that represents the Company Code field (BUKRS) in SAP authorization objects

When the ruleset is evaluated, $BUKRS gets populated with actual company code values from the user's authorizations or role assignments

 

Role details:

Role 1: Z_TEST_ROLE_1008

 

Role 2: Z_TEST_ROLE_1000

 

 

Assign this to a test user and run the Repo sync job ( SA38 ->GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC )

 

Step 2: Setting up Organization Rule

From the SAP NetWeaver Business Client, navigate to

  • Setup → Under Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules → Create

  • Select the relevant Risk ID for which you want to apply Organizational Rule.

  • Always Generate the Risk after creation or modification of Org Rule. ( Tcode: GRAC_GENERATE_RULES )

 

Step 3: Running Risk Analysis

From the SAP NetWeaver Business Client, navigate to

  • Setup → Under Access Risk Analysis → User Level

  • Input all the relevant Analysis Criteria

 

When we run Risk analysis without considering Org Rule, we see that the values are displayed as $BUKRS which leads to potential false positives

 

 

When we run Risk analysis considering Org Rule, we see that the values are displayed for BUKRS field which helps determine that this risk is clearly false positive and can be ignored.

 

Org Rule ID helps filter out these false positive results and focus on actual risks.

 

Conclusion:

Key Benefits

  1. False Positive Elimination

    • Filters out SoD conflicts that appear risky at the transaction level but are safe due to organizational restrictions

    • Prevents reporting of violations when users have conflicting access across different organizational boundaries (e.g., different company codes)

  2. Precise Risk Tracking and Management

    • Provides unique identification for each organizational rule, enabling targeted application to specific risks

    • Allows granular control over which SoD risks should consider organizational filtering

  3. Enhanced Audit Trail and Compliance

    • Creates a clear reference system for documenting why certain risks were filtered out during analysis

    • Supports regulatory compliance by providing justifiable reasons for risk exceptions based on organizational controls

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Hidden Gems – Fine-Tuning Fraud Detection Strategies in SAP BIS
entry.content:
By its very nature, fraud is dynamic. So, a static strategy quickly becomes less accurate over time.

Designing effective fraud detection strategies is challenging because fraud tactics evolve constantly, because the data to monitor is massive and comes in high-volume, and because fraud detection systems must strike a careful balance between identifying fraud and minimizing false positives—all while operating in real time and complying with privacy and regulatory requirements.

An effective fraud detection strategy typically combines various rules together, extended with machine learning for anomaly detection, to address both known fraud patterns and new, emerging threats.

As a result, ongoing performance monitoring and regular fine-tuning is essential to make sure the company adapts to changes in behaviour, evolving processes, and regulatory landscape.

And the secret ingredient to this successful recipe is the “regular fine-tuning”.

 

What is fine-tuning fraud detection strategies

 

In essence, this relates to adjusting an existing fraud model or set of fraud detection strategies, so it works better for an organization’s specific business and current fraud patterns.

This can be achieved by changing model weights, thresholds, creating and combining new detection methods, or adjusting fraud rule sensitivity based on new outcomes and investigator feedback. It is usually a continuous cycle: review performance, identify misses and false alerts, adjust, test, then redeploy.

Fine-tuning keeps the fraud system in equilibrium – robust enough to detect fraud while remaining flexible enough to avoid overwhelming investigators with excessive false alerts.

And this is where I would like to introduce a hidden gem embedded within SAP Business Integrity Screening since the very first day, but is not known widely enough in my humble opinion, the Calibration of Detection Strategies.

 

Calibration of Detection Strategies in SAP Business Integrity Screening

 

Let’s first take just one step back.

In case you are not familiar with SAP Business Integrity Screening, this solution helps organizations mitigate fraud risk, reduce losses, and improve the detection and prevention of anomalies. The short video below will provide a quick overview:

With this solution, fraud investigation teams can create detection methods with associated thresholds, weighing factors, etc. and then combine them into detection strategies. These then run automatically to surface anomalies that can then be further checked.

To make sure that these detection strategies are optimum, users can leverage the “Calibration of Detection Strategies” capability. This is a real-time simulation function for what-if analyses.

Step 1 – Review actual

In this first step, users get details on the baseline reference strategy to improve:

  • Number of confirmed, false positives, and unclassified alerts
  • Efficiency ratio of the detection strategy

Step 2 – Fine-tune the settings

In this second step, users can change settings such as:

  • Selection period for the data that is relevant for the simulation
  • Threshold data point of the strategy
  • Weighing factors and parameter values of each detection method
  • … and run the simulation

This will create a new tile with a new calculated efficiency ratio based on the following performance indicators:

 

KPI

Description

Confirmed

Number of alert items that would have been created and classified as Confirmed for detection objects that already have been identified as Confirmed in the actual data.

False Positives

Number of alert items that would have been created and classified as Confirmed for detection objects that already have been identified as False Alarm in the actual data.

New Alert Items

Number of alert items that will be created during simulation and that have not been created within the actual data.

Missed Alert Items

Number of alert items that were created by the strategy in the past but were not found during the current simulation.

Found Alert Items

Number of alert items that were both created by the strategy in the past and were found during the current simulation.

Risk Value of New Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the new alert items found during the current simulation that were not found by the strategy in the past.

Risk Value of Found Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the alert items that were both created by the strategy in the past and were found during the current simulation.

Risk Value of Missed Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the alert items that were created by the strategy in the past but were not found during the current simulation.

[optional] Step 3 – Get best fit suggestions

Of course, users can run as many simulations as they like until they get the best outcome for their requirements: reduce the number of positives, not miss any alerts, etc.

Should users also want some automated proposal, they can use the Find Best Values capability available. The system will then suggest the best values determined.

Step 4 – Roll it out

Once the detection strategy is adjusted to a satisfactory level, users can save the new set of settings.

By saving the results of the calibration, the system creates a new detection strategy version with status inactive. It would then simply need to be activated so that it can be executed routinely.

 

See it in action

 

 

Benefits of fine-tuning detection strategies

 

Reduces false positives so fraud investigators can focus on what matters

Improves detection method with adaptive measures as behaviours or tactics change, which keeps the strategy relevant over time

 

Interested in learning more?

 

In addition to the section on the SAP Help Portal, there is also an SAP Learning Course focused exclusively on this solution – Exploring SAP Business Integrity Screening, with a dedicated section on “Monitoring and Improving Detection Quality”:

What about you, how does your company ensure that its detection strategies continue to perform well over time? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Driving the Future of Water: SAP Brings Together Water Industry Leaders in Madrid
entry.content:
The water sector is at a transformative crossroad. Utilities and public service organizations worldwide are navigating increasing pressure to modernize operations, strengthen resilience, and deliver smarter, more sustainable services to communities. To meet this moment, SAP is organizing the Executive Value Network (EVN) for Water & Wastewater, next October 21-22 in Madrid as an international knowledge sharing oriented forum for those interested in digital transformation best practices of Water and Wastewater companies.

An Event Built on Strong Partnerships

Hosted by Canal de Isabel II, the Water Company of Madrid  and one of Europe’s most recognized water utilities, it will provide the ideal setting for a gathering of this significance. Farys, the Gent based Belgian water, sewage and municipal services company, will as a strategic advisor, contributing deep sector expertise and a collaborative vision for the future.

Complementing a strong ecosystem of support, Minsait and SEW – Smart Energy and Water join as partners, bringing complementary capabilities in digital transformation, technology integration, and water sector innovation. Together, this network of hosts and partners reflects the very spirit of the event: that progress in the water industry is best achieved through collaboration.

Two Keynotes Not to Be Missed: Canal de Isabel II and ANDA

At the heart of the event program will be two exceptional keynote sessions, each telling a compelling and distinct story about digital transformation in the water sector across very different contexts. Together, they demonstrate that while every utility’s journey is unique, the common purpose is universal: delivering better, more sustainable water services to people and communities.

Representing the host utility, María González Cano from Canal de Isabel II will open with a keynote that offers a mature, large-scale example of how to embed digital capabilities into the very heart of water operations and governance. As one of Europe’s leading metropolitan water utilities, Canal de Isabel II’s keynote sets the tone for the entire event — illustrating what is possible when digital ambition meets operational discipline.

Dagoberto Arévalo, CEO, and Dennis Barrientos, CIO of the Administración Nacional de Acueductos y Alcantarillados (ANDA) from El Salvador will bring a different, but equally powerful perspective to the stage. As a national water authority in an emerging economy, ANDA’s Digital Transformation Journey story is one of determination, pragmatism, and impact — demonstrating that digital transformation is a global imperative, not the exclusive domain of large European utilities by executing their 2025-2030 strategic plan.

 

A Rich Agenda: Three Focused Tracks for Every Stage of the Journey

Beyond the keynotes, the event agenda is structured around three main tracks, designed to help participants move from strategic vision to concrete action, regardless of where they are on their digital journey.

 

Strategic Asset Management: From a Technical Approach to a Citizen-Centric Perspective

Sessions will explore how to build a single source of truth for assets by bringing together technical, financial, operational, and service data; how to apply predictive analytics and risk-based planning to prioritize investments where they matter most for customers and communities; and how to connect asset strategies to resilience, sustainability, and quality of service.

The key shift this track champions is simple but powerful: start from citizens’ needs and work backwards into technology and operations — not the other way around.

This track will include an inspirational session which tackles one of the most fundamental questions in water management: how do you plan and invest in infrastructure so that real people receive reliable, safe, and affordable water services?

Maria Gonzalez (Canal Isabel II) and Inge Opreel (Farys)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Smart Metering

The smart metering track examines how digital measurement and connectivity can transform both operations and customer experience. Topics include architectures for Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) and their integration with SAP back-end systems, turning meter data into actionable insights for leak detection and demand forecasting, enabling new tariff models based on high-frequency consumption data, and managing organizational change as billing, field operations, and customer care become increasingly data-centric.

Sovereign Cloud Strategies

This track addresses one of the most strategic questions facing utilities today: how to embrace cloud innovation while ensuring data protection, compliance, and control. Sessions will cover the concept of sovereign cloud and why it matters for public and critical infrastructure operators, SAP’s approach to supporting data residency, security, and regulatory requirements, and hybrid migration strategies that allow utilities to innovate without disrupting mission-critical operations.

 

Research and Innovation: A Window into What Comes Next

Complementing the three main tracks, the agenda also features a dedicated Research and Innovation stream — designed for those who want to look beyond today’s best practices and anticipate tomorrow’s challenges.

This stream will showcase R&D projects, pilots, and collaborations between utilities, universities, startups, and technology partners. Topics will include AI and machine learning for water operations, digital twins, real-time water quality monitoring, and circular economy initiatives. It is a unique opportunity to discover new ideas and connect with innovators who are already shaping the next generation of water management

 

Networking, Dinner, and Hands-On Experiences

Great events are not only built in the conference room. This program has been designed to foster informal exchange, real-world learning, and lasting connections beyond the formal sessions.

Joint Dinner Sponsored by Minsait: On the evening of the first day, Minsait will host a joint dinner for all participants — a chance to continue the day’s conversations in a more relaxed and convivial atmosphere, explore potential collaborations, and build the kind of relationships that make a real difference back home.

Site Visit on Day Two: Control Center (CPC) and Metrology Laboratory: The second day features an exclusive site visit organized by Canal de Isabel II, offering participants a behind-the-scenes look at how digital strategies translate into real operations, this is connecting what is discussed in the room with what is delivered in the field.

  • Control Center (CPC): See how the Madrid water system is monitored and managed in real time — including how operational data, alarms, and analytics support decision-making and incident response across one of Europe’s largest urban water networks.
  • Metrology Laboratory: Discover how metering devices are tested, calibrated, and certified, and how robust metrology practices underpin reliable billing, smart metering projects, and overall data quality.

 

 

 

 

 

 



Whether you are a utility executive, a digital transformation leader, or a technology innovator, this event offers a unique opportunity to connect with peers, learn from world-class organizations like Canal de Isabel II and ANDA, and explore how SAP’s solutions can help your organization take its next step forward.

We look forward to welcoming you to Madrid.

To register or learn more, visit: https://events.sap.com/evn-water2026/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Why SAP ChaRM Still Matters in the S/4HANA Era: Lessons from Large-Scale Transformation Programs
entry.content:
Why SAP ChaRM Still Matters in the S/4HANA Era: Lessons from Large-Scale Transformation Programs

SAP ChaRM Change Lifecycle

Introduction

In many SAP transformation programs, project teams focus heavily on system design, data migration, testing, and cutover planning. However, one critical capability often receives attention only after deployment issues begin to appear: Change and Release Management.

Over the years, I have worked on multiple large SAP transformation programs where thousands of transport requests moved across development, quality, and production environments. While organizations invest heavily in implementation methodologies, many still underestimate the operational importance of a structured transport governance framework.

This is where SAP Solution Manager Change Request Management (ChaRM) continues to play a significant role.

Although SAP Cloud ALM is becoming increasingly popular, many organizations running ECC and SAP S/4HANA environments still depend on ChaRM to maintain governance, compliance, auditability, and release control.

In my experience, the difference between a stable deployment and a chaotic production rollout often comes down to how effectively change management processes are implemented.

What is SAP ChaRM?

SAP Change Request Management (ChaRM) is a capability within SAP Solution Manager that helps organizations manage the lifecycle of system changes from request initiation through deployment.

ChaRM provides:

• Change Request Tracking

• Transport Management

• Approval Workflows

• Release Governance

• Audit Compliance

• Emergency Change Management

• Deployment Documentation

Instead of manually tracking transports across spreadsheets, emails, and meetings, ChaRM creates a centralized governance framework that controls how changes move through the SAP landscape.

The Challenge Most SAP Programs Face

During a global SAP S/4HANA transformation, hundreds of developers, functional consultants, integration teams, and testing teams may be working simultaneously.

Without a structured governance process, organizations frequently encounter:

• Unauthorized transports

• Incomplete testing

• Emergency fixes bypassing controls

• Conflicting releases

• Missing approvals

• Audit findings

• Production instability

These issues are rarely caused by technology limitations. They are usually process and governance failures.

A Real Program Lesson

During one enterprise transformation program, multiple workstreams were preparing for a major release involving:

• Finance

• Supply Chain

• Manufacturing

• Procurement

• Integration platforms

As deployment approached, transport volume increased significantly.

Without proper release governance, several transport dependencies were identified only days before production deployment.

The project team spent considerable effort reconciling transport conflicts, identifying missing approvals, and validating deployment sequences.

The lesson was clear:

The risk was not the transport itself.

The risk was the lack of visibility into the overall change landscape.

Organizations need a governance framework that provides traceability from requirement to deployment.

This is precisely the problem ChaRM was designed to solve.

Why ChaRM Matters for SAP S/4HANA Programs

1. Improved Governance

Every transport becomes associated with:

• Business requirement

• Approval records

• Testing evidence

• Deployment documentation

This creates complete traceability across the implementation lifecycle.

2. Stronger Audit Compliance

Many regulated industries require evidence that production changes followed approved processes.

ChaRM automatically captures:

• Approvals

• Change history

• Deployment records

• Transport ownership

This significantly simplifies audit preparation.

3. Better Release Management

Large transformation programs often deploy multiple releases simultaneously.

ChaRM helps teams:

• Bundle related transports

• Manage release schedules

• Reduce deployment conflicts

• Improve release predictability

4. Emergency Change Control

Production incidents inevitably occur.

The challenge is balancing speed with governance.

ChaRM supports emergency transport processes while maintaining visibility and auditability.

This helps organizations resolve critical issues without sacrificing compliance.

Common Mistakes Organizations Make

Over the years, I have observed several recurring mistakes:

Treating ChaRM as an IT Tool

Successful organizations treat ChaRM as a governance framework, not merely a transport management solution.

Excessive Customization

Overengineering approval workflows often introduces unnecessary complexity.

The objective should be governance efficiency, not process bureaucracy.

Ignoring Business Ownership

Change governance should involve both IT and business stakeholders.

Production changes ultimately impact business operations, not just technical systems.

Delaying ChaRM Implementation

Many programs wait until testing phases to implement change governance.

By that stage, transport volume and complexity have already increased significantly.

ChaRM vs Cloud ALM

As SAP continues investing in Cloud ALM, organizations frequently ask whether ChaRM remains relevant.

The answer depends on organizational maturity, landscape complexity, and regulatory requirements.

For many enterprises running hybrid environments, ChaRM continues to provide robust governance capabilities that remain critical for large-scale deployments.

The focus should not be selecting a tool.

The focus should be establishing a disciplined change management process that supports reliable business operations.

Final Thoughts

Technology transformations succeed when governance evolves alongside technology.

Organizations invest millions in SAP S/4HANA programs, yet production stability often depends on something far less visible: disciplined change management.

SAP ChaRM may not be the most discussed component of an SAP transformation, but in many programs it serves as the foundation that connects requirements, testing, approvals, transports, and production deployment.

From my experience, successful transformation programs do not simply manage transports.

They manage change.

And that distinction often determines whether a deployment becomes a success story or a post-go-live recovery effort.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Digital Compliance Monitoring Cockpit with SAP Risk and Assurance Management
entry.content:
Enterprise Risk and Compliance cockpit image generated by AI

Leading risk and compliance in any organization can sometimes feel like flying a fighter jet straight into a storm with no way of avoiding it. And your mission is to ensure that the plane goes through it without damages and continues its course smoothly.

Daunting, right? But there are upsides: 1. You are not alone since the entire organization – including Senior Management, is onboard, and 2. Contrarily to early wooden planes, yours can benefit from instant data available at your fingertips. Preventing you from finding out that one tank is burst and fuel leaking – slowly but surely – only once the gauge turns to critical red…

 

What makes a good cockpit?

 

When looking at how engineers design plane cockpits, they consider multiple dimensions of course, but with one output in mind: it needs to deliver the right information, at the right time, in the simplest possible way – while enabling immediate action under stressful conditions.

How does that compare to SAP Risk and Assurance Management might you say? Well, let’s continue this analogy!

 

Situational awareness

 

The pilot – here the 2nd line of defense composed of Risk Managers, Compliance Officers, Cybersecurity leads, etc., must understand everything happening around them (compliance coverage, control gaps, cyber threats) instantly.

SAP Risk and Assurance Management helps achieves this by combining data from various systems to show a unified and coherent view of compliance status. Regardless of the compliance initiative: financial governance, IT Security, tax checks, etc.

Controls Overview dashboard app with view of various key metrics of controls

 

Information prioritization

 

At Mach 3, information overload leads to paralysis or worse even, mistakes. The same applies to an organization.

SAP Fiori apps in SAP Risk and Assurance Management have been specifically designed to display only critical data with easy to grasp symbology (icons, colours) to make it obvious what needs to be done.

Manage Risks app

 

Head-Up Display

 

Granted not many Compliance Officers have 3D helmet systems in their office to visualise their compliance progress – but that would be pretty cool, key data is directly in the line of sight with advanced reporting. No need to look at the ground – or Excel sheet – to know at what altitude you are.

Internal control, compliance and risk management information rendered in SAP Analytics Cloud

 

Hands-On Throttle-And-Stick

 

Maybe not as critical in an office than in a plane, but still, the office version of the stick controller is the… mighty computer mouse!

Less clicking, more doing. Critical details are available directly on the line records in the dedicated apps. This reduces the need to search for information so enables users to act rapidly and decisively.

My Controls app

 

High level of automation

 

A digital co-pilot, this is precisely what this is. Alleviating the workload so that Compliance and Risk specialists – but also Senior Management when using this information, can turn their attention to tactics and decision-making. As a result, the system must behave autonomously and manage non-critical tasks itself. These can include categorizing and closing false alerts so that users can spend effort on resolving real issues requiring attention and expertise.

Predicted Conclusions for Issue Management and Remediation

 

Integration of all systems

 

To achieve the automation mentioned in the previous paragraph, there is one prerequisite: everything must work as one system, not separate components. Only then can true automation take place. Navigation, communication, and sensors must be fully integrated so that there is no need to manually reconcile different sources.

Simplified diagram of integration points in SAP Risk and Assurance Management

 

Clear and immediate alert

 

As for a pilot, business process owners must react rapidly to threats and danger. Increasing risk level of course but also control deficiency fit well in this category. Early warnings with real time notification categorized by severity provide them with timely information to address the underlying issues. Alerts in the tool of course, but also sent to the beloved email inboxes.

Email notification with direct link to the record

 

Rapid drill-down capability

 

Should an alert be raised because a control has failed, it is critical to seamlessly transition from awareness to action. Users must be able to quickly navigate from an overview to the underlying details of the inconsistency and initiate the appropriate remediation steps without delay. This ability to navigate efficiently from insights to action ensures that risks are addressed promptly, and compliance is maintained in a continuous and controlled manner.

Direct access to the live source system data for root cause resolution

 

Reliability and redundancy

 

For external stakeholders, process checks performed in SAP Risk and Assurance Management might not be perceived as critical. But when they realize that these control checks relate to compliance, then the perception shifts. This information must remain available for auditors and regulators, but also for Senior Management to support their decision making and provide traceability in case of investigation. Here, backups and fail-safe help prevent failures.

In this area, “SAP cloud solutions provide contractually agreed-upon service-level agreements (SLAs) for resilience and recovery. Customers can enhance these baselines SLAs by selecting premium options appropriate to their business requirements and threat models, including high availability and disaster recovery deployment options” (Securing SAP Cloud Environments)

SAP Trust Center landing page

SAP Risk and Assurance Management enables organizations to continuously oversee risks, detect process deficiencies and compliance issues instantly, and act where it matters most.

As for cockpits in fighter jets, in both cases success depends on integrating critical information into a clear, real-time, and actionable control centre.

What about you, how does your organization design its compliance cockpit? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: GRC Game: AI vs Human. Result: 6:0. Six months of production SAP GRC Firefighter data study.
entry.content:
Final whistle on six months of SAP Firefighter activity in a real production landscape. Team Human Review: 0. Team AI Agent: 6. Team AI did not win because it was faster than the human side. It won because it played a different game on the same field. This article series walks through what those six “goals” were, how Team AI scored them, and why the score is the more process and approach fault, not necessary so called human errors. The six “goals” scored by AI Agents during the game described across this series:

  • Goal #1. The FF executor vs auditor relationship (peer review audit)
  • Goal #2. The source-of-FF activity (technical accounts under emergency labels)
  • Goal #3. GDPR exposure during FF session (technical field)
  • Goal #4. Missing data in approved FF session (approval judgement)
  • Goal #5. FF emergency account used for mass changes
  • Goal #6. The quality of FF session review (missing critical transaction usage)

This article walks through the six "goals" in order, then the post-match analysis: how Team AI was built, the six-layer architecture behind it, the validation discipline, and the one-week actions Team Human can take to compete on the same field starting Monday.

By Filip Nowak, GRC Advisory. The six signals described in this blog were produced by a GRC AI agent in the GRC LAB on an anonymized extract of a real production SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment. Methodology, heuristics, and editorial responsibility rest with the GRC Advisory consulting team.

What this blog is, and is not

This is a data-pattern study, split into three parts for readability. It analyzes an anonymized, production-scale SAP Firefighter activity dataset and describes six classes of control signal visible when the data is examined cross-FF sessions instead on traditional (oriented on one FF activity review). It is not a public audit report. It is not a description of weaknesses at a specific organization. It is not a claim that AI replaces human auditors.

What it does claim: that classical session-by-session Firefighter review is not designed to surface cross-session, cross-user, cross-source patterns; that those patterns are quantifiable in production data of normal scale; and that a GRC AI agent equipped with the right architecture can extract those patterns reliably enough that human auditors can validate and act on them. Every signal was checked, on the underlying data, by an accountable GRC security consultant before it appeared in this series.

The agent detects patterns. The human decides whether the pattern is audit-relevant.

The match-up

Two ways to read a Firefighter session. Session-based review asks: was this individual session legitimate? This is the question every classical Firefighter audit review is structured to answer. The FF reviewer opens the session, reads the reservation reason, scans the transaction log, checks for obvious anomalies, signs off. That is the Team Human playbook.

AI pattern-based analysis started as typical Human review but also additionally asks more and go deeper with different type of question: does the arrangement of sessions, users, sources, transactions, audit statuses and historical baselines creates a risk pattern? This requires cross-referencing data domains the session-based review does not surface together: relationship graphs between requesters and approvers, source-host distributions across accounts, regex-style content of accounting document header text, system-parameter configuration governing change-document logging, concentration ratios for activity per user, dwell-time distributions per transaction risk class. That is the Team AI playbook.

Same data. Same field of play. Two different playbooks. The signals described in this series are visible only when you ask more and different type of questions. The data to answer it exists in tables every SAP and GRC landscape already maintains. The AI review insight does not exist in classical session review. At least so far and untill now. That is the asymmetry that turned the match into AI victory with score 6 to 0.

The match conditions

Six months of continuous observation on a production SAP S/4HANA landscape with multiple company codes. Almost 600 Firefighter reservations across 11 emergency accounts, 39 unique end users. Approximately 2.55 million Security Audit Log entries. Around 352,000 master-data changes. Over 201,000 accounting documents. Approximately 14,000 SAP audit log entries per day.

To be explicit on data origin: the dataset is real production data extracted from a real SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment, not a synthetic or test environment. References to “our LAB” point to the analytical environment in which the GRC AI agent processed the anonymized extract, not to the origin of the data. Specific identifiers, account names, country structure, and exact date ranges have been anonymized; orders of magnitude and structural patterns are unchanged.

The volume context matters for the ~600 FF sessions in this dataset. At the typical 5-10% sampling rate, the most qualified and engaged FF log reviewers examine maybe 30 to 60 sessions in detail - spending an enormous amount of time and effort on the activity, trying to find patterns of wrongdoing inside each FF session. The other 530-plus get an 'accept' click. Sampling at 5-10% is not a process choice - it is a resource constraint. At any larger sample size, the audit population would need to expand by an order of magnitude, and the controls that justified keeping it narrow (independence, accountability, audit-trail integrity) would not hold. No reviewer working session-by-session could ever have spotted the first two patterns described below: they only emerge when you compute across the whole dataset. The math and structure of the review do not allow it. The review was not designed to ask the question. This was the field on which Team Human played.

Goal #1. The FF executor vs auditor relationship (peer review audit)

The question this signal answers: when the same population of users both reserves and approves Firefighter sessions, what fraction of accepted sessions form closed loops where heavy users approve each other?

The dataset showed 115 sessions in six months, roughly one in four accepted sessions, were approved by an auditor who themselves was among the heaviest Firefighter operators. Both the requester and the approver belonged to the same operational team in most of these cases. Each individual approval, in isolation, looks defensible. The pattern emerges only by computing the user-auditor graph across all accepted sessions and identifying the closed loops.

Why this matters: a reviewer auditing a teammate's session operates under different incentives than one auditing a stranger. Trust is faster than challenge. Pushing back on a colleague's session creates two costs the reviewer cannot easily absorb. First, friction inside the team that has to keep working together. Second, the reasonable expectation that the same ‘detailed’ inspection will come back the next time their own session is in the queue. Challenge goes uphill. Acceptance goes downhill. The path of least resistance is to sign and move on.

Each individual approval still looks defensible. Process was followed. The requester is authorized to reserve. The auditor is authorized to clear. The trail is clean. On the surface, one person works and another verifies, which reads like control. The systemic risk lives only in the aggregate, where the people most able to scrutinize each other's work also have the least incentive to do so. That picture is not visible in any single session record.

How AI scored this goal: it stopped looking at individual session records and started looking at the network behind them. The agent built a relational model of the entire audit cycle. Every requester as a node. Every approver as a node. Every accepted session as a directed edge from requester to approver. With that graph in working memory, finding the closed loops where heavy users sign off on each other's sessions is a single query, not a 481-record scan. The AI agent also cross-referenced the graph with two contextual layers that the manual workflow keeps in separate tools: the authorization matrix that says who can approve, and the per-user activity baseline that identifies the heavy-user population. The pattern emerged in seconds because the agent was looking at the network structure, not at individual records. That is the move that scored the goal as FF quality and independent review of activity during the FF session is a fundament of this control. No quality review = no control for this risk is in place.

Why Team Human's session-based playbook could not match this: the log entries exist inside the review, but no view inside the workflow lets the reviewer aggregate them by source host across accounts. Aggregating that classification per account and comparing the ratio against a baseline for emergency-versus-technical accounts requires the role catalogue and a heuristic about what proportion suggests technical use. Neither is part of the per-session checklist. The reviewer has the data. They do not have the lens. It also requires the reviewer to think beyond the immediate checklist and ask a different kind of question: not only “was this individual session justified?”, but “what pattern emerges when these sessions are compared across accounts, hosts, and account types?” That is a deeper analytical step than the standard workflow encourages. To be fair, this kind of task is especially difficult for a human reviewer because it involves reading through long review logs for hours, often containing repetitive, low-signal entries. The work is time-consuming, monotonous, and mentally draining. After a certain point, attention naturally drops, and subtle patterns become easy to miss. A reviewer may correctly process each individual entry and still fail to notice the broader signal hidden across many similar sessions. In other words, the limitation is not that the reviewer lacks access to the evidence. The limitation is that the evidence is buried inside a FF workflow optimized for per-session validation, not cross-account pattern detection. The data is present, but the review process does not make the relevant pattern visible.

Goal #2. The source-of-FF activity (technical accounts under emergency labels)

The question this signal answers: what proportion of activity on a given Firefighter account originates on a server hostname versus a user workstation, and which accounts behave as de facto service accounts despite their emergency-access classification?

The dataset showed that 53.7% of all Security Audit Log entries from Firefighter sessions, approximately 1.37 million lines, originated on server hostnames. That is automation running under accounts labeled as emergency access, not human emergency work. Three of the eleven Firefighter accounts showed a source-host distribution dominated by server traffic, indicating they function as de facto technical accounts. The structural recommendation: replace those three accounts with narrowly scoped technical accounts following the Z_BC_* pattern.

On a per-session level, no individual session reveals this pattern. Each session shows a reservation, a list of transactions, and an audit verdict. The fact that the transactions inside the session originated on a server hostname rather than the requester's workstation is buried in a column the audit workflow never groups by. A reviewer who opens the session sees activity that looks legitimate: transactions matching the role scope, a reasonable time window, a clean log. The structural anomaly does not appear at the session level. It is visible only when you classify the log entries by source host and aggregate the result per account. That classification is not in the per-session view.

How AI scored this goal: it stopped reading the audit log as a list of session events and started reading it as a dimensional dataset. The agent computed the source-host distribution across all 2.55 million log entries, broken down by Firefighter account. Within seconds it had a table of eleven accounts, each with a ratio of server-host to workstation activity. Then it cross-referenced that table with two things the manual workflow keeps in separate places: the role catalog (which account is what type) and the SAP industry heuristic (more than half server-host activity equals a service-account workload, not an emergency-access workload). Three Firefighter accounts stood out as behaving like technical accounts in disguise. The agent then produced the structural recommendation: reclassify those three accounts to the Z_BC_* pattern with narrow scope, not "tell the reviewer to look closer next time". That is the move that scored the goal.

Why Team Human's session-based playbook could not match this: the log entries exist inside the review, but no view inside the workflow lets the reviewer aggregate them by source host across accounts. Aggregating that classification per account and comparing the ratio against a baseline for emergency-versus-technical accounts requires the role catalogue and a heuristic about what proportion suggests technical use. Neither is part of the per-session checklist. The reviewer has the data. They do not have the lens.

Goal #3. GDPR exposure during FF session (technical field)

The question this signal answers: do fields designated for system references contain personally identifiable information that should live elsewhere?

AI Agent review of the dataset showed 19 posted accounting documents in which the BKPF header text field contained customer email addresses on consumer mail providers. Header text on a financial document is designed for system references: invoice IDs, internal correlation keys, document chain pointers. Customer contact data appearing there is a GDPR concern. Personal data is now living in a system field that has no retention policy of its own, is not registered in the organization’s Records of Processing Activities as a PII repository, and is regularly exported in standard reporting flows including audit submissions and partner data shares.

What is actually broken: BKPF-BKTXT is officially classified as “technical reference field” in the data inventory. That classification means DSAR queries (GDPR Art. 15) skip it, erasure requests (Art. 17) skip it, retention policies skip it, and pseudonymisation rules in data exports skip it. Nineteen customer email addresses are now embedded in a 5-plus year accounting record that nobody scans for personal data, in a field nobody looks at when honoring a customer’s data rights. The organization cannot demonstrate compliance with GDPR Art. 5, 15, 17, or 30 for this data class, and does not know that it cannot.

Why per-session FF review did not catch this: when an auditor opens an FF session and reviews the posted documents, they see header text as a string. Even if they mentally register that an entry looks like a customer contact, the audit checklist for FF session review has no item that says “if you see a PII-shaped string in BKPF-BKTXT, raise a control finding”. The reviewer has no follow-up action defined. The field is classified as technical reference, so anything appearing there is treated as a technical reference by default. Human failed to identify this as a problem not because of inattention, but because the review protocol does not assign GDPR compliance scanning to the FF audit step, and because awareness that this information should not be disclosed in posted documents is GDPR consultant knowledge, not part of the standard FF reviewer training.

How AI scored this goal: it ran a content-pattern scan that the manual FF review does not include. The agent took the full set of BKPF-BKTXT values from FF-posted documents, tens of thousands of strings, and applied a regex pattern library covering email addresses, phone numbers, identification numbers, and other personal-data formats. Within seconds it had 19 documents flagged. Then it applied a GRC consultant heuristic that the standard checklist does not encode: when personal data appears in fields not registered as PII repositories, surface it as a control gap and link it to the FF session that posted it. The agent then connected the finding to GDPR Art. 5 (data minimisation), Art. 15 (right of access), Art. 17 (right to erasure), and Art. 30 (records of processing), producing not just nineteen documents but a defensible control statement an internal auditor can take to the DPO. That is the move that scored the goal. PII handling under FF is a control area GDPR-compliant operations must demonstrably manage. No targeted scan of free-text fields means no evidence that this control is operating.

Why Team Human could not score against this: no human auditor reviewing FF sessions one at a time runs a regex match for PII patterns across the BKPF header text field, and the per-session review surfaces individual strings but does not aggregate them for scanning. The heuristic that says “when personal data appears in fields designated for system references, surface it as a control gap” is part of senior GRC consultant practice mapped to specific GDPR articles, not part of the standard FF audit checklist. The reviewer has the strings in front of them. They do not have the scanner, the PII pattern library, or the GDPR-article mapping that would make the strings actionable as a compliance finding.

Goal #4. Missing data in approved FF session (approval judgement)

The question this signal answers: is the FF audit log capturing enough detail to support post-hoc verification of mass-data changes, and if not, where does the gap actually live?

The dataset showed that for approximately 297,000 material master changes performed by the highest-volume FF user, the details field in the CDPOS change-document entries was empty. Not partial. Not garbled. Empty. The standard interpretation of this gap is that mass-data operations do not produce field-level change documents, and that detail-level verification is therefore impossible after the fact.

What is actually broken: ITGC frameworks (SOX, ISO 27001 A.12.4) require that changes to data driving financial outcomes be traceable at field level. 297,000 changes logged as “unable to verify” is not a partial gap; it is a complete failure of field-level change control over the largest single block of master-data activity in the cycle. An auditor cannot conclude operating effectiveness on a control where 297,000 evaluation entries return “unable to verify”. Yet the per-session review treats each entry as a known limitation, the working note stays “unable to verify”, and the cumulative finding is never raised at platform level.

The deeper interpretation, surfaced on a feedback-loop pass of the analysis, is that this is not a logging behavior problem at user level. It is a system parameter configuration problem at platform level. The SAP parameter governing field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW updates is most likely not enabled in the landscape. The fix is not “tell auditors to review more carefully”. The fix is in SAP configuration. Nobody noticed or requested it, because asking for it would make an already-impossible review task harder. Under conditions where 300,000 entries are sitting in the queue, the review quality drops by volume alone. Even working 24/7 the verification cannot be completed. It is hard to expect anyone to ask for even more data to be captured there. It is easier to “accept” the review with the gap and move the next session forward.

How AI scored this goal: it reframed the problem with help from a feedback-loop pass. On the first pass through the dataset, the agent flagged the empty details field as a verification gap, the same interpretation a human reviewer would reach. On a later pass, with the heuristic layer enhanced by a GRC consultant correction, the agent asked a different question: if the gap is structural (it repeats across 297,000 changes from one user), what platform-level setting controls field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW operations? It cross-referenced SAP Basis configuration parameters, which the manual FF review does not include in its checklist, and identified that the relevant parameter is most likely not enabled. The agent then connected the finding to SOX ITGC and ISO 27001 A.12.4 logging requirements, producing not just a session-level note but a platform-level control finding internal audit can route to the Basis team for remediation. That is the move that scored the goal. FF audit cannot demonstrate the operating effectiveness of mass-data change controls without field-level capture being enabled. No config means no audit evidence means no defensible control.

Why Team Human could not score against this: auditors had logged the empty-details field in their working notes as “unable to verify” across many sessions. Correlating that observation with the SAP configuration parameter that controls field-level audit logging requires both SAP Basis expertise and an audit-control framing. Both sit in the consultant heuristics layer, not in the per-session reviewer’s checklist. The reviewer has the symptom in front of them. They do not have the Basis knowledge plus the framing to convert “I cannot verify” into “the configuration choice is the control gap, and here is the parameter”.

The fix is not “tell auditors to review more carefully”. The fix is in system configuration.

Goal #5. FF emergency account used for mass changes

The question this signal answers: how is Firefighter activity distributed across the user population over six months, and does any single profile’s work pattern match a service-account signature rather than a human power-user signature?

The dataset showed one user profile accounted for 36% of all Firefighter Security Audit Log entries, 85% of all FF master-data changes (299,017 records), and 99.8% of all FF-posted accounting documents (201,413 of 201,840). Three independent measures of FF activity, all pointing to the same profile. The math is not subtle.

What is actually broken: FF is by definition emergency access. Exceptional. Time-limited. Reservation-based. If one profile executes 99.8% of all FF accounting activity in a six-month window, the access is not emergency for that profile. It is permanent elevated access in disguise. The compensating control built on the assumption “FF equals exception” stops being a control the moment FF equals routine. SoD limitations in the user’s normal role no longer apply to the actual workload, because the actual workload runs through the FF path. SOX and ITGC audit treats “emergency access used for routine operations” as an automatic control deficiency. ISO 27001 A.9.2.5 (Review of user access rights) requires privileged accounts be reviewed against actual need. A profile generating 99.8% of accounting documents under FF clearly needs standing access, not emergency access, but the review process has no mechanism to convert that need into a standing-role provisioning decision.

The work pattern itself matches a batch-interface signature, not a human power-user signature. High volume across hundreds of thousands of transactions. Repetitive transaction codes within a narrow scope. No exploratory clicks. No transaction-failure entries. Predictable execution windows. Either a human is executing 200,000-plus transactions manually, which is physically impossible, or some form of automation is running under this human credential through the FF access. Both interpretations are control gaps. The organization either does not know it is running batch automation through emergency access, or it knows and has accepted that emergency access is the daily operational path for one of its most active users.

Why this concentration was never raised: nobody designed it. It accumulated over years, month by month. Year one this user did perhaps 30% of master-data work because they were the most experienced. Year two 50% because newer team members deferred to them. Year three 85% because by then nobody else remembered how to do it. By the time someone looks at the six-month aggregate, the concentration is a single-point dependency dressed up as expertise. The team labels this person an irreplaceable senior contributor. The audit population labels them a heavy user, reviewed correctly. The FF review process has no metric called “your share of cycle volume”, and no signal that says this concentration is mathematically inconsistent with a human power-user profile. The drift is invisible at any single review cycle, and the social framing of “she or he is our master-data expert” protects it from being questioned.

How AI scored this goal: it computed concentration ratios across multiple dimensions of FF activity (log entries per user, master-data changes per user, accounting documents per user), and compared the resulting distribution against a library of operational signatures. Within seconds it had a ranking placing this single profile at 36%, 85%, and 99.8% across the three measures. Then it applied a GRC consultant heuristic the manual FF review does not encode: when a single profile’s distribution matches a batch-interface signature (high volume, narrow transaction range, repeatable cadence, near-zero failure rate, predictable windows), reclassify it as a service-account candidate, not a heavy user. The agent produced the structural recommendation: build a standing technical role with Z_BTC_* narrow scope and migrate this workload out of FF entirely. It connected the finding to SOX ITGC (emergency access used for routine operations is a deficiency), ISO 27001 A.9.2.5 (privileged access review), and ISO 22301 (business continuity, since single-person dependency for 99.8% of accounting throughput is a documented continuity risk). That is the move that scored the goal. Three control frameworks point to the same fix: take this workload off FF, put it on a standing technical role, restore FF to its exception path.

Why Team Human could not score against this: each session opened by this user, reviewed in isolation, looks like legitimate operational work. Transactions match the role scope. Reservation reasons reference real tickets. The audit verdict is “accept”. Nothing in any individual session reveals that the same person is responsible for 99.8% of all FF accounting documents in the dataset. Concentration ratios are a property of the entire dataset, not of any one session. The session log does not carry “your contribution to the cycle aggregate is 99.8%”. Even when reviewers know intuitively that this person is “always on FF”, quantifying that intuition requires computing per-user-share metrics across multiple activity dimensions and comparing the result against a library of batch-interface signatures. Neither is part of per-session review. The reviewer sees the volume. They do not see the concentration ratio, and they do not have the pattern library to distinguish heavy-user volume from automation-shaped volume.

The fix is not “review the heavy user more carefully”. The fix is to recognize batch-interface workload for what it is and remove it from FF entirely.

Goal #6. The quality of FF session review (missing critical transaction usage)

The question this signal answers: do FF sessions containing transactions of differing risk class receive differentiated handling in the review queue, or does a high-risk transaction wait the same number of days for review as a low-risk one?

The dataset showed nine sessions executed transaction F110 (Payment Run, which generates real bank payment orders that move money out of the company the same day). Six of those nine were still in PENDING audit status weeks or months after the session closed. In this dataset, the F110 pattern concentrated around one finance profile. Total F110 executions across the nine sessions: 23. Number of those 23 independently reviewed by the time of analysis: zero. Across the broader dataset, the dwell time of high-risk transactions in PENDING exceeded the dwell time of low-risk ones, the inverse of what an audit policy would intend.

What is actually broken: F110 is the SAP transaction that generates real bank payment orders. Each execution authorizes an outbound payment that leaves the company’s account the same business day. ITGC frameworks (SOX, COBIT) classify payment authorization as a primary control. ISO 27001 A.9.4.4 explicitly addresses control over privileged utility programs, and F110 is a textbook example. Twenty-three F110 executions with zero independent review means the four-eyes principle around outbound payments is not operating. The control on paper says “F110 sessions require timely independent review”. The control in practice says “F110 sessions sit in PENDING longer than routine sessions because nobody volunteers to be the second pair of eyes on a real bank payment”.

Why per-session FF review let high-risk sessions sit longer: it is a risk-aversion paradox. The mental cost of clearing an F110 session is asymmetric. A wrongly cleared F110 review attaches the auditor’s name to a real bank payment that should not have happened. A deferred F110 review costs nothing visible. The rational individual response, given that asymmetry, is to defer until “I have time to do it properly”. But every reviewer makes the same calculation, on the same day, about the same kind of session. The cohort behavior produces an inverted SLA without anyone in the cohort intending it. Add to that the operational truth that F110 sessions take longer to review because the analysis is heavier (more transactions, more amounts, more invoice references to check), so they linger. And the queue does not surface the cohort effect to any individual reviewer.

How AI scored this goal: it correlated transaction risk class with review-status duration across the entire backlog. The agent built a distribution of “days in PENDING” segmented by the highest-risk transaction in the session (F110, SE38, SU01, SM30, all known high-risk codes), and compared it against the distribution for routine sessions. The result was the inversion: high-risk sessions averaged longer dwell time than low-risk ones. Then the agent applied a GRC consultant heuristic the manual checklist does not encode: high-risk transactions must have shorter SLA in PENDING, not longer. The agent produced the structural recommendation: add a transaction-risk-class field to the FF review queue ordering, auto-escalate sessions containing high-risk codes to a four-eyes review track with a defined SLA, route them out of the default PENDING queue, and report SLA compliance separately. It connected the finding to SOX ITGC (payment authorization control), ISO 27001 A.9.4.4 (privileged utility programs), and the four-eyes principle. That is the move that scored the goal. Bank payments executed under FF without timely independent review is exactly the control failure auditors look for in external assessment. No risk-class routing means F110 sits in the same queue as F-04, and the highest-risk activity gets the least time.

Why Team Human could not score against this: each session is reviewed when an auditor opens it. The review queue does not communicate to the auditor whether this session is more or less time-critical than others in the queue, nor what the cohort-level SLA pattern looks like. The dwell-time distribution is not visible inside any session, it is a property of the queue, and the queue does not surface it. Even when an individual reviewer knows that F110 sessions tend to wait too long, they have no quantitative signal to escalate, and no mechanism to route the F110 queue differently from the routine queue.

The fix is not “tell auditors to clear F110 sessions faster”. The fix is to route them out of the default queue and into a four-eyes track with a defined SLA.

Six goals scored. Before turning to the post-match analysis of how Team AI was actually built, it is worth being explicit about what the agent deliberately did not surface.

The goals Team AI did not score

Worth surfacing what the agent did not surface. It did not flag any individual session as fraudulent. The dataset contained no evidence of bad actors.
