It did not invent compliance findings that were not there. It did not propose remediations the policy layer does not support. It did not, despite running on a model that is fully capable of hallucination, hallucinate transactions, document numbers, or user activity. The numbers in this series appear because the agent counted directly from CDPOS, BKPF and similar tables, not because it estimated.

This is the practical answer to the standard objection to AI in audit contexts. Hallucination happens when a model is asked to reason about something it has no grounded data for.

The agent in this experiment had grounded data for everything it said. The heuristics layer told it what to look for. The tool layer let it pull verified counts from the underlying platform. The feedback layer from previous runs corrected its biases. The output the agent could not ground in the data, it did not surface.

This is not the same as a hallucination-free guarantee. It is a verifiability guarantee: every count the agent did surface is reproducible by re-running the same query against the same tables.

The model is the smallest part of an AI agent in audit. The architecture around it is what determines whether the output is defensible.

How Team AI trains: methodology and validation

The heuristics layer the agent reasons over was authored by senior GRC consultants with 15+ years of SAP GRC AC audit experience, peer-reviewed against the firm’s audit training material, and version-controlled. Each heuristic must reference a documented audit-control rationale before it is committed to the agent’s reasoning library. Heuristics are not added by prompt iteration; they are added by the same process by which a consulting firm would write a new internal control.

Every signal the agent produced was reviewed by a human GRC AC consultant before it appeared in this series. Two candidate signals from earlier analysis passes were rejected in review: one was a false positive based on incomplete role-catalogue data (the agent had been working with a stale snapshot from before a routine SoD redesign), and one was a real anomaly that did not have audit-relevant implications and would have produced noise rather than signal. The six signals published all passed that human review.

False-positive rate across all analysis passes ran at approximately 8%. Roughly one in twelve agent-flagged items was rejected by the human reviewer as either based on incomplete context or as a benign outlier. The agent’s confidence score correlates with reviewer acceptance, but is not a substitute for review. Every signal in this series was checked, on the underlying data, by an accountable GRC consultant. No signal is presented as definitive without that human sign-off.

The division of labour is the principle. The agent detects patterns at a scale and across data domains that human review cannot reach: pattern detection across 600 sessions, ratio computation across 2.55 million log entries, cross-referencing across multiple data domains. The human decides whether the pattern is audit-relevant, whether the framing is correct, and whether it is publishable as a control claim. Neither replaces the other.

How Team AI lines up: the six-layer architecture

A tool layer: each function of the GRC platform exposed as a well-typed API the agent can call without hallucinating its arguments. Every action bounded, type-checked, and reversible.

A policy layer: Firefighter rules, SoD ruleset, transaction risk classes, audit comment templates, four-eyes routing rules. All in a structured format the agent queries, rather than free-text the model has to interpret.

A historical context layer: every past audit verdict with its comment, every reservation reason with its outcome, indexed and queryable.

A heuristics layer: the GRC consultant know-how about real anomalies versus benign outliers. The most difficult layer to build, because the authors are senior consultants, not LLM engineers.

A feedback layer: corrections from previous runs that adjust the agent’s bias on the next pass.

And on top of all of it, a deterministic verification step that re-runs every count the agent produces, so the output is reproducible end-to-end on demand.

This is what AI in a GRC platform means as a product category. Not a ChatGPT box added to the UI. The same audit functions the platform already has, exposed as agent tools, sitting under layers of business-audit logic and historical context that structurally constrain hallucination and make every agent claim verifiable.

A different vendor’s GRC platform (SAP GRC, others in this class) could build the same architecture. In our implementation, these capabilities are delivered through the smartAccess module of smartGRC. The architecture is platform-neutral.

The discipline of keeping the agent grounded in verifiable data, rather than marketing the hallucination problem away, is what will define the next five years of SAP GRC.

How Team Human gets back into the game

Measure your own pitch

The signals are reproducible. They live in data your organization already has, in tables your GRC platform already queries. The following questions can be answered against your own Firefighter dataset with the same tool layer the agent in this experiment used: change-document tables, accounting document headers, audit log entries, reservation history, role catalogues. For the relationship signal: of all Firefighter sessions accepted in your last cycle, what fraction were approved by a member of your top decile of heavy users?

For the relationship signal (Goal #1): build a directed graph of FF reservations where each requester is a node, each approver is a node, and every accepted session is an edge from requester to approver. What fraction of accepted sessions form closed loops where heavy FF users sign off on each other’s sessions?

For the source-of-activity signal: per Firefighter account, what is the ratio of audit log entries originating on server hostnames versus user workstations? Which accounts cross the threshold that suggests service-account workload?

For the data-placement signal: a regex search across BKPF header text for the last six months. How many documents contain email addresses or other personal data formats?

For the logging-completeness signal: is the SAP system parameter controlling field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW operations enabled in your landscape?

For the concentration signal: per Firefighter user profile, what is their share of total accounting documents and master-data changes?

For the review-priority signal (Goal #6): in your current PENDING queue, what is the dwell-time distribution of sessions containing F110, SE38, SU01, or SM30 versus the dwell time of routine sessions?

Each of these queries is a one-week task for a competent SAP/GRC analyst. The patterns they surface, if any, will not be the same numbers as in this series. Different landscapes produce different distributions. The questions are the same.

Three one-week actions

The signals translate into concrete one-week actions for different roles in the GRC operation, independent of any structural multi-year change program. A CISO should commission a source-of-activity audit on the organization’s own Firefighter accounts. A one-day query against the Security Audit Log will produce the same kind of percentage as Goal 2, or a different one. If the number is high, some emergency accounts may be operating as technical accounts in disguise.

A GRC Manager should add a transaction-risk-class field to the audit workflow’s PENDING queue ordering, mapping Goal 6 to a review-queue configuration change rather than a project. The Firefighter policy you wrote already classifies F110 as high-risk; the workflow needs to read that classification.

An internal auditor should request from the SAP Basis team confirmation of whether the field-level change-document parameter (Goal 4) is enabled in the landscape, and treat the answer as a control-evaluation finding either way.

Each of these is a one-week action, not a multi-year program.

The bigger match

The question is not whether to put AI in GRC. The question is whether GRC platforms will expose their audit functions as agent tools the way they exposed them as human UIs ten years ago, and whether the heuristics GRC consultants carry in their heads will be encoded into a context layer an agent can reason over consistently. The first vendors to do that, with the discipline to keep the agent grounded in verifiable data rather than marketing the hallucination problem away, will define what the next five years of SAP GRC look like.

Whether your audit cycle finds these patterns before they appear in an external auditor’s report depends entirely on which tools you give the people responsible for finding them. The signals in this series were the agent’s first deliverable. The structural fixes they point at are now everyone’s problem to solve.

About this series. The dataset analyzed in this article comes from a real production SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment; the analysis was conducted by GRC Advisory’s smartGRC LAB on an anonymized extract of that data. The article does not name, identify, or attribute the analysis to any specific organization. If you operate SAP Firefighter at scale and want to discuss running a similar analysis on your own data, please comment the research and study.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Moving Beyond Automation - Agentic AI for Risk, Compliance, and Audit
entry.content:
Risk, Compliance and Audit teams have long operated under competing pressures: mounting regulatory demands, siloed data, and manual processes that consume time better spent on judgment. Traditional GRC automation isn't enough anymore. Traditional workflow automation follows predefined rules, while risks evolve faster than those rules can be updated. At the same time, compliance monitoring is becoming increasingly continuous, requiring systems that can analyze, reason, and act.

Agentic AI provides the missing layer between traditional automation and truly continuous governance.

 

The shape of Autonomous GRC

 

The shift from reactive to proactive compliance isn't simply a technology upgrade, it's a fundamental change in what's possible.

When AI agents handle the repetitive, high-volume, time-sensitive work of GRC, human expertise gets applied where it creates the most value: judgment, escalation, and strategic risk management.

Unlike earlier automation tools that required rigid rules and predefined workflows, AI agents reason, adapt, and act across complex processes—from detecting anomalies to drafting audit-ready documents. For GRC, agents don't just speed up existing work; they make continuously vigilant oversight possible at a scale no human team can match.

As a result, we're building a suite of AI agents for SAP GRC to do exactly that—coordinated by a central orchestrator called the Governance Assistant.

 

The Governance Assistant: your GRC command center

 

The Governance Assistant serves as the AI control center for GRC operations, serving as the intelligence layer that coordinates specialized agents, enterprise risk data, compliance activities, controls, and audit execution

It connects Governance, Risk, and Compliance teams to AI agents that monitor risks, streamline compliance workflows, surface control exceptions, and turn complex governance data into clear, actionable insights – faster than traditional manual approaches allow.

Whether identifying open control deficiencies, summarizing emerging regulatory changes, or highlighting unresolved audit issues, the Governance Assistant turns fragmented governance data into actionable insight.

What makes the Governance Assistant distinctive is its built-in human-in-the-loop design. AI and people work together – the assistant accelerates analysis and surfaces recommendations, while your team retains control over decisions that matter.

This balance means faster authorization requests, sharper visibility into regulatory changes, improved audit quality, and quicker remediation when issues arise. In practice, use cases span from breaking down siloed compliance monitoring to automating evidence collection for audits, optimizing access request workflows, and surfacing control irregularities before they escalate.

 

Internal Audit Agent: from announcements to audit programs in minutes [Planned for Q3 2026]

 

Audit lifecycles are effort-intensive, language-dependent, and time-consuming to coordinate. The Internal Audit Agent reimagines this from the ground up and boosts audit productivity.

When an audit announcement arrives—in any language—the Internal Audit Agent automatically detects the language, translates the content, classifies the audit type, assigns the right team, and sets up the audit plan. It then generates a complete work program from the attached questionnaire, matching incoming questions semantically against a growing knowledge base of prior answers and clustering them into scopes and question procedures. What used to take days – intake, translation, clustering, and team assignment – now happens in minutes.

The agent supports any audit type: tax, compliance, IT, and more. It even drafts audit reports and response letters automatically, translating back into the original announcement language.

Every completed audit enriches the knowledge base, so matching accuracy and work program quality improve continuously. For global teams, language barriers disappear. For auditors, that means less time on logistics and more time on the questions that genuinely require human judgment.

For sneak peak, here’s an early overview video:

 

Integrity Screening Insights Agent: transparent, auditable screening [Planned for Q3 2026]

 

Screening business partners against sanctions lists, politically exposed persons databases, adverse media, and state-owned enterprise registers is a time-intensive compliance obligation. And the consequences of errors can be severe!

The Integrity Screening Insights Agent automates this process within SAP Business Integrity Screening – and more SAP solutions will be connected in the future to provide transparency and explainability to compliance teams.

It collects and aggregates evidence across multiple data sources automatically, using configurable risk dimensions and country or industry-specific analysis profiles. Each screening result comes with a transparent confidence score that shows exactly how each dimension contributed: weighted evidence sources, freshness indicators, and conflict detection across sources:

This matters for audit trails. Compliance analysts don't just get a result, they get an explanation. They can see why a score is high, which sources drove it, and whether the underlying data is current. That transparency makes AI-generated results defensible, internally but also to regulators.

Critically, the Integrity Screening Insights Agent works alongside existing address list screening rather than replacing it, adding intelligence without disrupting established compliance controls.

 

Compliance Agent: control validation at scale [Planned for Q4 2026]

 

Traditional compliance monitoring works in cycles with periodic reviews, point-in-time audits, and after-the-fact testing. The gaps between checkpoints are where risk lives.

The Compliance Agent changes the operating model entirely. Instead of periodic testing, it continuously monitors, tests, and validates internal controls – automating the manual Test of Control Effectiveness process and executing test cases for manual controls automatically.

Repetitive monitoring tasks move off your team's plate, freeing capacity for higher-value analysis. Bottlenecks in compliance workflows disappear because continuous automation doesn't wait for the next review cycle.

The cost equation also shifts: by demonstrating ongoing compliance monitoring to regulators, organizations can improve audit readiness and reduce effort associated with external audits, and lower insurance premiums and liability exposure through stronger demonstrated compliance.

Beyond cost, there's a risk quality dimension: consistent and systematic testing can reduce human variability and improve coverage, anomalies surface early before they escalate, and compliance policies are enforced consistently across all business units. The result is a documented, continuous evidence trail that makes every future audit faster and more straightforward to close.

 

And more to come!

 

This is only the beginning. Additional agents are already being planned across the broader GRC landscape. Looking ahead, Agentic AI will also reshape Enterprise Risk Management. Future capabilities could continuously monitor key risk indicators, detect emerging risks from internal and external signals, suggest risk assessments, and help risk managers understand how evolving threats impact strategic objectives.

 

Trusted and Responsible AI at SAP: What GRC Professionals Need to Know

 

SAP defines Responsible AI around three explicit pillars — ethics, security, and compliance — and states that every AI system it creates must be grounded in ethical principles, comply with applicable law (including the EU AI Act), and maintain technical robustness and data security. This is not aspirational language: it is governed through an SAP Global AI Ethics Policy that is binding for all SAP employees involved in AI development, deployment, use, and sale.

Organizations that put trusted and responsible AI automation at the center of their GRC operations can experience significant benefits:

While organizations remain accountable for governance and compliance decisions, AI agents can significantly reduce the operational burden associated with monitoring, testing, and documentation.

Provided they are adequately governed. Agentic AI in GRC requires clear governance boundaries. Organizations must determine which activities agents can execute autonomously, which require approval, how evidence is retained, and how decisions remain explainable and auditable.

With this in place, organizations will shift compliance from reactive cycle-based work to continuous, AI-assisted oversight and lay the foundation for continuous assurance across risk, controls, compliance, and audit activities

The Governance Assistant orchestrates a set of purpose-built AI agents with Audit Agent, Integrity Insights Agent, and Compliance Agent already planned and more to come, each targeting a specific high-effort, high-risk area of the GRC lifecycle. Together, they shift compliance from reactive cycle-based work to continuous, AI-assisted oversight.

What about you, what is your strategy to alleviate workload from your GRC teams? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!
